It typically houses a drum inside and is carried or pulled in accordance with the rhythm of the drum. There are two main types: '''Kakiyama''' (舁き山, carried floats) and '''Hikiyama''' (曳き山, pulled floats).
== Overview of Taikodai == [[File:甲八幡神社 秋祭り 本宮 甲池に並ぶ屋台.jpg|thumb|View of portable shrine roof floats (taikodai) lined up at {{ill|Kōhachiman Shrine|ja|甲八幡神社}} in [[Himeji]], Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[File:Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine Mikoshi.JPG|thumb|Taikodai ({{ill|portable shrine entry|ja|宮入り}}) at {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}} in [[Miki]], Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[File:Niihamataikomatsuri02.jpg|thumb|Taikodai at the {{ill|Niihama Taiko Festival|ja|新居浜太鼓祭り}} (part of the {{ill|Three Great Fighting Festivals|ja|日本三大喧嘩祭り}}) in {{ill|Niihama|ja|新居浜市}}, Ehime Prefecture]] [[File:Nakamozu-cho Futon-Daiko 002.webm|thumb|Taikodai (Futon Daiko) in [[Nakamozu]], [[Sakai]], Osaka Prefecture]]
Taikodai are especially common along the {{ill|Seto Inland Sea|ja|瀬戸内海}} region and throughout western Japan. While their specific designs vary by region, the general practice involves 2–6 people entering the float to play the drum inside, while tens to over a hundred people carry it, shouting rhythmic chants as they parade. Alternate names include '''Taiko''', '''Futon Taiko''', '''Chōsa''', '''Chōsai''' (頂載), '''Senzairaku''' (千歳楽), '''Yassa''', '''Yotsutaiko''' (四つ太鼓), '''Futon Mikoshi''' (布団御輿), '''Mikoshi Taiko''', '''Futon Dashi''' (布団だんじり), '''Taiko Yama''' (太鼓山), and others. As a collective term, they are also called '''Yatai''' (屋台) or '''Taiko Yatai''' (太鼓屋台).
Unlike {{ill|mikoshi|ja|神輿}}, which are owned by the shrine, taikodai are usually provided by the community and serve to escort or lead the mikoshi during a {{ill|Shinkōsai|ja|神幸祭}}, as well as to offer performances before the shrine as an act of {{ill|devotion|ja|奉納}}.
=== Origins and Chants === The name "taikodai" highlights the drum's central role in the procession. The drumming pattern and accompanying chants vary by region but often follow rhythmic patterns such as "Don, Dododon, Dododon, Dododon, Dendon (and repeat)." Common chants include: - "Chōsaja" (or "Yoissaja") - "Sōrya, Sōrya" - "Yossai, Yossai" - "Yōsaja, Yoiyoisaja" - "Bērabēra, Berasshossho"
The dragon, symbolizing rain-bringing deities or messengers of the gods, is often featured in taikodai decoration. The fabric straps used to secure the "futon" (mattress-like tiers) atop the float are embroidered with golden dragons, and tassels on the straps symbolize rain. Thus, in regions like {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|ja|香川県}} and eastern {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|ja|愛媛県}}, taikodai have historically been associated more with rainmaking rituals than with mikoshi.
While taikodai do not carry divine spirits like mikoshi, some regions consider the futon or roof portions as sacred vessels where spirits may reside. For this reason, women were traditionally prohibited from touching taikodai in certain areas. Boys who play the drums inside may dress elaborately and avoid touching the ground, much like traditional {{ill|Chigo|ja|稚児}}.
Taikodai are distinct from hikiyama floats in that they are carried rather than pulled. This makes them easier to maneuver in mountainous areas or over uneven terrain. They are often raised high overhead in displays known as "sashiage" (差し上げ) and may also be shaken, rotated, or competed in lifting contests.
== Structure == [[File:Nadanokenkamatsuri-yatai42.jpg|thumb|150px|Interior drum of a taikodai at the {{ill|Nada Fighting Festival|ja|灘のけんか祭り}}]] [[File:大阪 堺 西湊 ふとん太鼓.jpg|thumb|200px|Futon Taiko from {{ill|Sakai|ja|堺市}}, Osaka Prefecture]]
- The base, called the "taiwa" (台輪), supports the drum, carrying poles, railings, and vertical pillars, with a roof of futon or heavy tiers. - The futon (重) tiers vary from 2 to 8 in number, depending on regional tradition. - Ornamental features such as tassels, decorative straps, and embroidered dragons are common. - Modern taikodai may use lightweight materials for ease of transport.
For further details on specific styles, see: - {{ill|Futon Taiko|ja|ふとん太鼓|1=Futon Daiko}} - {{ill|Chōsa|ja|頂載}}

The fabric straps used to secure the "futon" (mattress-like tiers) atop the float are embroidered with golden dragons, and tassels on the straps symbolize rain. Thus, in regions like {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|ja|香川県}} and eastern {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|ja|愛媛県}}, taikodai have historically been associated more with rainmaking rituals than with mikoshi.
While taikodai do not carry divine spirits like mikoshi, some regions consider the futon or roof portions as sacred vessels where spirits may reside. For this reason, women were traditionally prohibited from touching taikodai in certain areas. Boys who play the drums inside may dress elaborately and avoid touching the ground, much like traditional {{ill|Chigo|ja|稚児}}.
Taikodai are distinct from hikiyama floats in that they are carried rather than pulled. This makes them easier to maneuver in mountainous areas or over uneven terrain. They are often raised high overhead in displays known as "sashiage" (差し上げ) and may also be shaken, rotated, or competed in lifting contests.
== Structure == [[File:Nadanokenkamatsuri-yatai42.jpg|thumb|150px|Interior drum of a taikodai at the {{ill|Nada Fighting Festival|ja|灘のけんか祭り}}]] [[File:大阪 堺 西湊 ふとん太鼓.jpg|thumb|200px|Futon Taiko from {{ill|Sakai|ja|堺市}}, Osaka Prefecture]]
- The base, called the "taiwa" (台輪), supports the drum, carrying poles, railings, and vertical pillars, with a roof of futon or heavy tiers. - The futon (重) tiers vary from 2 to 8 in number, depending on regional tradition. - Ornamental features such as tassels, decorative straps, and embroidered dragons are common. - Modern taikodai may use lightweight materials for ease of transport.
For further details on specific styles, see: - {{ill|Futon Taiko|ja|ふとん太鼓|1=Futon Daiko}} - {{ill|Chōsa|ja|頂載}}

{{Citation needed|date=March 2019}} '''Taikodai''' (太鼓台) refers to:
1. A platform used to place a {{ill|taiko|ja|太鼓}} drum. 2. A type of {{ill|festival float|ja|山車}} used in {{ill|shrine|ja|神社}} festivals. It typically houses a drum inside and is carried or pulled in accordance with the rhythm of the drum. There are two main types: '''Kakiyama''' (舁き山, carried floats) and '''Hikiyama''' (曳き山, pulled floats).
== Overview of Taikodai == [[File:甲八幡神社 秋祭り 本宮 甲池に並ぶ屋台.jpg|thumb|View of portable shrine roof floats (taikodai) lined up at {{ill|Kōhachiman Shrine|ja|甲八幡神社}} in [[Himeji]], Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[File:Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine Mikoshi.JPG|thumb|Taikodai ({{ill|portable shrine entry|ja|宮入り}}) at {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}} in [[Miki]], Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[File:Niihamataikomatsuri02.jpg|thumb|Taikodai at the {{ill|Niihama Taiko Festival|ja|新居浜太鼓祭り}} (part of the {{ill|Three Great Fighting Festivals|ja|日本三大喧嘩祭り}}) in {{ill|Niihama|ja|新居浜市}}, Ehime Prefecture]] [[File:Nakamozu-cho Futon-Daiko 002.webm|thumb|Taikodai (Futon Daiko) in [[Nakamozu]], [[Sakai]], Osaka Prefecture]]
Taikodai are especially common along the {{ill|Seto Inland Sea|ja|瀬戸内海}} region and throughout western Japan. While their specific designs vary by region, the general practice involves 2–6 people entering the float to play the drum inside, while tens to over a hundred people carry it, shouting rhythmic chants as they parade. Alternate names include '''Taiko''', '''Futon Taiko''', '''Chōsa''', '''Chōsai''' (頂載), '''Senzairaku''' (千歳楽), '''Yassa''', '''Yotsutaiko''' (四つ太鼓), '''Futon Mikoshi''' (布団御輿), '''Mikoshi Taiko''', '''Futon Dashi''' (布団だんじり), '''Taiko Yama''' (太鼓山), and others. As a collective term, they are also called '''Yatai''' (屋台) or '''Taiko Yatai''' (太鼓屋台).
Unlike {{ill|mikoshi|ja|神輿}}, which are owned by the shrine, taikodai are usually provided by the community and serve to escort or lead the mikoshi during a {{ill|Shinkōsai|ja|神幸祭}}, as well as to offer performances before the shrine as an act of {{ill|devotion|ja|奉納}}.
=== Origins and Chants === The name "taikodai" highlights the drum's central role in the procession. The drumming pattern and accompanying chants vary by region but often follow rhythmic patterns such as "Don, Dododon, Dododon, Dododon, Dendon (and repeat)." Common chants include: - "Chōsaja" (or "Yoissaja") - "Sōrya, Sōrya" - "Yossai, Yossai" - "Yōsaja, Yoiyoisaja" - "Bērabēra, Berasshossho"
The dragon, symbolizing rain-bringing deities or messengers of the gods, is often featured in taikodai decoration. The fabric straps used to secure the "futon" (mattress-like tiers) atop the float are embroidered with golden dragons, and tassels on the straps symbolize rain. Thus, in regions like {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|ja|香川県}} and eastern {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|ja|愛媛県}}, taikodai have historically been associated more with rainmaking rituals than with mikoshi.
While taikodai do not carry divine spirits like mikoshi, some regions consider the futon or roof portions as sacred vessels where spirits may reside. For this reason, women were traditionally prohibited from touching taikodai in certain areas. Boys who play the drums inside may dress elaborately and avoid touching the ground, much like traditional {{ill|Chigo|ja|稚児}}.
Taikodai are distinct from hikiyama floats in that they are carried rather than pulled. This makes them easier to maneuver in mountainous areas or over uneven terrain. They are often raised high overhead in displays known as "sashiage" (差し上げ) and may also be shaken, rotated, or competed in lifting contests.
== Structure == [[File:Nadanokenkamatsuri-yatai42.jpg|thumb|150px|Interior drum of a taikodai at the {{ill|Nada Fighting Festival|ja|灘のけんか祭り}}]] [[File:大阪 堺 西湊 ふとん太鼓.jpg|thumb|200px|Futon Taiko from {{ill|Sakai|ja|堺市}}, Osaka Prefecture]]
- The base, called the "taiwa" (台輪), supports the drum, carrying poles, railings, and vertical pillars, with a roof of futon or heavy tiers. - The futon (重) tiers vary from 2 to 8 in number, depending on regional tradition. - Ornamental features such as tassels, decorative straps, and embroidered dragons are common. - Modern taikodai may use lightweight materials for ease of transport.
For further details on specific styles, see: - {{ill|Futon Taiko|ja|ふとん太鼓|1=Futon Daiko}} - {{ill|Chōsa|ja|頂載}}

{{Citation needed|date=March 2019}} '''Taikodai''' (太鼓台) refers to:
1. A platform used to place a {{ill|taiko|ja|太鼓}} drum. 2. A type of {{ill|festival float|ja|山車}} used in {{ill|shrine|ja|神社}} festivals. It typically houses a drum inside and is carried or pulled in accordance with the rhythm of the drum. There are two main types: '''Kakiyama''' (舁き山, carried floats) and '''Hikiyama''' (曳き山, pulled floats).
== Overview of Taikodai == [[File:甲八幡神社 秋祭り 本宮 甲池に並ぶ屋台.jpg|thumb|View of portable shrine roof floats (taikodai) lined up at {{ill|Kōhachiman Shrine|ja|甲八幡神社}} in [[Himeji]], Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[File:Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine Mikoshi.JPG|thumb|Taikodai ({{ill|portable shrine entry|ja|宮入り}}) at {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}} in [[Miki]], Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[File:Niihamataikomatsuri02.jpg|thumb|Taikodai at the {{ill|Niihama Taiko Festival|ja|新居浜太鼓祭り}} (part of the {{ill|Three Great Fighting Festivals|ja|日本三大喧嘩祭り}}) in {{ill|Niihama|ja|新居浜市}}, Ehime Prefecture]] [[File:Nakamozu-cho Futon-Daiko 002.webm|thumb|Taikodai (Futon Daiko) in [[Nakamozu]], [[Sakai]], Osaka Prefecture]]
Taikodai are especially common along the {{ill|Seto Inland Sea|ja|瀬戸内海}} region and throughout western Japan. While their specific designs vary by region, the general practice involves 2–6 people entering the float to play the drum inside, while tens to over a hundred people carry it, shouting rhythmic chants as they parade. alternate_name include '''Taiko''', '''Futon Taiko''', '''Chōsa''', '''Chōsai''' (頂載), '''Senzairaku''' (千歳楽), '''Yassa''', '''Yotsutaiko''' (四つ太鼓), '''Futon Mikoshi''' (布団御輿), '''Mikoshi Taiko''', '''Futon Dashi''' (布団だんじり), '''Taiko Yama''' (太鼓山), and others. As a collective term, they are also called '''Yatai''' (屋台) or '''Taiko Yatai''' (太鼓屋台).
Unlike {{ill|mikoshi|ja|神輿}}, which are owned by the shrine, taikodai are usually provided by the community and serve to escort or lead the mikoshi during a {{ill|Shinkōsai|ja|神幸祭}}, as well as to offer performances before the shrine as an act of {{ill|devotion|ja|奉納}}.
=== Origins and Chants === The name "taikodai" highlights the drum's central role in the procession. The drumming pattern and accompanying chants vary by region but often follow rhythmic patterns such as "Don, Dododon, Dododon, Dododon, Dendon (and repeat)." Common chants include: - "Chōsaja" (or "Yoissaja") - "Sōrya, Sōrya" - "Yossai, Yossai" - "Yōsaja, Yoiyoisaja" - "Bērabēra, Berasshossho"
The dragon, symbolizing rain-bringing deities or messengers of the gods, is often featured in taikodai decoration. The fabric straps used to secure the "futon" (mattress-like tiers) atop the float are embroidered with golden dragons, and tassels on the straps symbolize rain. Thus, in regions like {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|ja|香川県}} and eastern {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|ja|愛媛県}}, taikodai have historically been associated more with rainmaking rituals than with mikoshi.
While taikodai do not carry divine spirits like mikoshi, some regions consider the futon or roof portions as sacred vessels where spirits may reside. For this reason, women were traditionally prohibited from touching taikodai in certain areas. Boys who play the drums inside may dress elaborately and avoid touching the ground, much like traditional {{ill|Chigo|ja|稚児}}.
Taikodai are distinct from hikiyama floats in that they are carried rather than pulled. This makes them easier to maneuver in mountainous areas or over uneven terrain. They are often raised high overhead in displays known as "sashiage" (差し上げ) and may also be shaken, rotated, or competed in lifting contests.
== Structure == [[File:Nadanokenkamatsuri-yatai42.jpg|thumb|150px|Interior drum of a taikodai at the {{ill|Nada Fighting Festival|ja|灘のけんか祭り}}]] [[File:大阪 堺 西湊 ふとん太鼓.jpg|thumb|200px|Futon Taiko from {{ill|Sakai|ja|堺市}}, Osaka Prefecture]]
- The base, called the "taiwa" (台輪), supports the drum, carrying poles, railings, and vertical pillars, with a roof of futon or heavy tiers. - The futon (重) tiers vary from 2 to 8 in number, depending on regional tradition. - Ornamental features such as tassels, decorative straps, and embroidered dragons are common. - Modern taikodai may use lightweight materials for ease of transport.
For further details on specific styles, see: - {{ill|Futon Taiko|ja|ふとん太鼓|1=Futon Daiko}} - {{ill|Chōsa|ja|頂載}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Nada Fighting Festival|ja|灘のけんか祭り}} * {{ill|Niihama Taiko Festival|ja|新居浜太鼓祭り}} * {{ill|Chōsa.net|ja|ちょうさドットＮＥＴ}}
{{Japanese festivals}} [[Category:Chōsa]][[Category:completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Taikodai]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Taikodai]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Taikodai]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Taikodai]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Japanese festivals]] [[Category:Chōsa]][[Category:completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Taikodai]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Taikodai]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Taikodai]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Taikodai]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Floats and portable shrines]] [[Category:Chōsa]][[Category:completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Taikodai]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Taikodai]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Taikodai]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Taikodai]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Chōsa]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Taikodai]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Taikodai]][[Category:11]][[sn:Taikodai]][[Category:11]][[sn:Taikodai]][[Category:11]][[sn:Taikodai]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Taikodai]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Taikodai]][[Category:11]][[sn:Taikodai]][[Category:11]][[sn:Taikodai]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Taikodai]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Taikodai]][[Category:11]][[sn:Taikodai]][[Category:11]][[sn:Taikodai]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Taikodai]][[Category:11]] [[ja:太鼓台]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:aikodai]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Taikodai]][[Category:11]][[sn:aikodai]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:aikodai]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Taikodai]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Taikodai]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Taikodai]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Taikodai]][[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Taikodai]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Taikodai]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Taikodai}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

{{Citation needed|date=March 2019}} '''Taikodai''' (太鼓台) refers to:
1. A platform used to place a {{ill|taiko|ja|太鼓}} drum. 2. A type of {{ill|festival float|ja|山車}} used in {{ill|shrine|ja|神社}} festivals. It typically houses a drum inside and is carried or pulled in accordance with the rhythm of the drum. There are two main types: '''Kakiyama''' (舁き山, carried floats) and '''Hikiyama''' (曳き山, pulled floats).
== Overview of Taikodai == [[File:甲八幡神社 秋祭り 本宮 甲池に並ぶ屋台.jpg|thumb|View of portable shrine roof floats (taikodai) lined up at {{ill|Kōhachiman Shrine|ja|甲八幡神社}} in [[Himeji]], Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[File:Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine Mikoshi.JPG|thumb|Taikodai ({{ill|portable shrine entry|ja|宮入り}}) at {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}} in [[Miki]], Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[File:Niihamataikomatsuri02.jpg|thumb|Taikodai at the {{ill|Niihama Taiko Festival|ja|新居浜太鼓祭り}} (part of the {{ill|Three Great Fighting Festivals|ja|日本三大喧嘩祭り}}) in {{ill|Niihama|ja|新居浜市}}, Ehime Prefecture]] [[File:Nakamozu-cho Futon-Daiko 002.webm|thumb|Taikodai (Futon Daiko) in [[Nakamozu]], [[Sakai]], Osaka Prefecture]]
Taikodai are especially common along the {{ill|Seto Inland Sea|ja|瀬戸内海}} region and throughout western Japan. While their specific designs vary by region, the general practice involves 2–6 people entering the float to play the drum inside, while tens to over a hundred people carry it, shouting rhythmic chants as they parade. alternate_name include '''Taiko''', '''Futon Taiko''', '''Chōsa''', '''Chōsai''' (頂載), '''Senzairaku''' (千歳楽), '''Yassa''', '''Yotsutaiko''' (四つ太鼓), '''Futon Mikoshi''' (布団御輿), '''Mikoshi Taiko''', '''Futon Dashi''' (布団だんじり), '''Taiko Yama''' (太鼓山), and others. As a collective term, they are also called '''Yatai''' (屋台) or '''Taiko Yatai''' (太鼓屋台).
Unlike {{ill|mikoshi|ja|神輿}}, which are owned by the shrine, taikodai are usually provided by the community and serve to escort or lead the mikoshi during a {{ill|Shinkōsai|ja|神幸祭}}, as well as to offer performances before the shrine as an act of {{ill|devotion|ja|奉納}}.
=== Origins and Chants === The name "taikodai" highlights the drum's central role in the procession. The drumming pattern and accompanying chants vary by region but often follow rhythmic patterns such as "Don, Dododon, Dododon, Dododon, Dendon (and repeat)." Common chants include: - "Chōsaja" (or "Yoissaja") - "Sōrya, Sōrya" - "Yossai, Yossai" - "Yōsaja, Yoiyoisaja" - "Bērabēra, Berasshossho"
The dragon, symbolizing rain-bringing deities or messengers of the gods, is often featured in taikodai decoration. The fabric straps used to secure the "futon" (mattress-like tiers) atop the float are embroidered with golden dragons, and tassels on the straps symbolize rain. Thus, in regions like {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|ja|香川県}} and eastern {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|ja|愛媛県}}, taikodai have historically been associated more with rainmaking rituals than with mikoshi.
While taikodai do not carry divine spirits like mikoshi, some regions consider the futon or roof portions as sacred vessels where spirits may reside. For this reason, women were traditionally prohibited from touching taikodai in certain areas. Boys who play the drums inside may dress elaborately and avoid touching the ground, much like traditional {{ill|Chigo|ja|稚児}}.
Taikodai are distinct from hikiyama floats in that they are carried rather than pulled. This makes them easier to maneuver in mountainous areas or over uneven terrain. They are often raised high overhead in displays known as "sashiage" (差し上げ) and may also be shaken, rotated, or competed in lifting contests.
== Structure == [[File:Nadanokenkamatsuri-yatai42.jpg|thumb|150px|Interior drum of a taikodai at the {{ill|Nada Fighting Festival|ja|灘のけんか祭り}}]] [[File:大阪 堺 西湊 ふとん太鼓.jpg|thumb|200px|Futon Taiko from {{ill|Sakai|ja|堺市}}, Osaka Prefecture]]
- The base, called the "taiwa" (台輪), supports the drum, carrying poles, railings, and vertical pillars, with a roof of futon or heavy tiers. - The futon (重) tiers vary from 2 to 8 in number, depending on regional tradition. - Ornamental features such as tassels, decorative straps, and embroidered dragons are common. - Modern taikodai may use lightweight materials for ease of transport.
For further details on specific styles, see: - {{ill|Futon Taiko|ja|ふとん太鼓|1=Futon Daiko}} - {{ill|Chōsa|ja|頂載}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Nada Fighting Festival|ja|灘のけんか祭り}} * {{ill|Niihama Taiko Festival|ja|新居浜太鼓祭り}} * {{ill|Chōsa.net|ja|ちょうさドットＮＥＴ}}

For further details on specific styles, see: - {{ill|Futon Taiko|ja|ふとん太鼓|1=Futon Daiko|12=draft}} - {{ill|Chōsa|ja|頂載|12=draft}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Nada Fighting Festival|ja|灘のけんか祭り|12=draft}} * {{ill|Niihama Taiko Festival|ja|新居浜太鼓祭り|12=draft}} * {{ill|Chōsa.net|ja|ちょうさドットＮＥＴ|12=draft}}
{{Japanese festivals}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Chōsa]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Japanese festivals]] [[Category:Floats and portable shrines]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[sn:Taikodai]] [[en:Taikodai]] [[ja:太鼓台]] [[sn:aikodai]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Taikodai}}

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=March 2019}} '''Taikodai''' (太鼓台) refers to:
1. A platform used to place a {{ill|taiko|ja|太鼓|12=draft}} drum. 2. A type of {{ill|festival float|ja|山車|12=draft}} used in {{ill|shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} festivals. It typically houses a drum inside and is carried or pulled in accordance with the rhythm of the drum. There are two main types: '''Kakiyama''' (舁き山, carried floats) and '''Hikiyama''' (曳き山, pulled floats).
== Overview of Taikodai == [[File:甲八幡神社 秋祭り 本宮 甲池に並ぶ屋台.jpg|thumb|View of portable shrine roof floats (taikodai) lined up at {{ill|Kōhachiman Shrine|ja|甲八幡神社|12=draft}} in [[Himeji]], Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[File:Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine Mikoshi.JPG|thumb|Taikodai ({{ill|portable shrine entry|ja|宮入り|12=draft}}) at {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)|12=draft}} in [[Miki]], Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[File:Niihamataikomatsuri02.jpg|thumb|Taikodai at the {{ill|Niihama Taiko Festival|ja|新居浜太鼓祭り|12=draft}} (part of the {{ill|Three Great Fighting Festivals|ja|日本三大喧嘩祭り|12=draft}}) in {{ill|Niihama|ja|新居浜市|12=draft}}, Ehime Prefecture]] [[File:Nakamozu-cho Futon-Daiko 002.webm|thumb|Taikodai (Futon Daiko) in [[Nakamozu]], [[Sakai]], Osaka Prefecture]]
Taikodai are especially common along the {{ill|Seto Inland Sea|ja|瀬戸内海|12=draft}} region and throughout western Japan. While their specific designs vary by region, the general practice involves 2–6 people entering the float to play the drum inside, while tens to over a hundred people carry it, shouting rhythmic chants as they parade. alternate_name include '''Taiko''', '''Futon Taiko''', '''Chōsa''', '''Chōsai''' (頂載), '''Senzairaku''' (千歳楽), '''Yassa''', '''Yotsutaiko''' (四つ太鼓), '''Futon Mikoshi''' (布団御輿), '''Mikoshi Taiko''', '''Futon Dashi''' (布団だんじり), '''Taiko Yama''' (太鼓山), and others. As a collective term, they are also called '''Yatai''' (屋台) or '''Taiko Yatai''' (太鼓屋台).
Unlike {{ill|mikoshi|ja|神輿|12=draft}}, which are owned by the shrine, taikodai are usually provided by the community and serve to escort or lead the mikoshi during a {{ill|Shinkōsai|ja|神幸祭|12=draft}}, as well as to offer performances before the shrine as an act of {{ill|devotion|ja|奉納|12=draft}}.
=== Origins and Chants === The name "taikodai" highlights the drum's central role in the procession. The drumming pattern and accompanying chants vary by region but often follow rhythmic patterns such as "Don, Dododon, Dododon, Dododon, Dendon (and repeat)." Common chants include: - "Chōsaja" (or "Yoissaja") - "Sōrya, Sōrya" - "Yossai, Yossai" - "Yōsaja, Yoiyoisaja" - "Bērabēra, Berasshossho"
The dragon, symbolizing rain-bringing deities or messengers of the gods, is often featured in taikodai decoration. The fabric straps used to secure the "futon" (mattress-like tiers) atop the float are embroidered with golden dragons, and tassels on the straps symbolize rain. Thus, in regions like {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|ja|香川県|12=draft}} and eastern {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|ja|愛媛県|12=draft}}, taikodai have historically been associated more with rainmaking rituals than with mikoshi.
While taikodai do not carry divine spirits like mikoshi, some regions consider the futon or roof portions as sacred vessels where spirits may reside. For this reason, women were traditionally prohibited from touching taikodai in certain areas. Boys who play the drums inside may dress elaborately and avoid touching the ground, much like traditional {{ill|Chigo|ja|稚児|12=draft}}.
Taikodai are distinct from hikiyama floats in that they are carried rather than pulled. This makes them easier to maneuver in mountainous areas or over uneven terrain. They are often raised high overhead in displays known as "sashiage" (差し上げ) and may also be shaken, rotated, or competed in lifting contests.
== Structure == [[File:Nadanokenkamatsuri-yatai42.jpg|thumb|150px|Interior drum of a taikodai at the {{ill|Nada Fighting Festival|ja|灘のけんか祭り|12=draft}}]] [[File:大阪 堺 西湊 ふとん太鼓.jpg|thumb|200px|Futon Taiko from {{ill|Sakai|ja|堺市|12=draft}}, Osaka Prefecture]]
- The base, called the "taiwa" (台輪), supports the drum, carrying poles, railings, and vertical pillars, with a roof of futon or heavy tiers. - The futon (重) tiers vary from 2 to 8 in number, depending on regional tradition. - Ornamental features such as tassels, decorative straps, and embroidered dragons are common. - Modern taikodai may use lightweight materials for ease of transport.
For further details on specific styles, see: - {{ill|Futon Taiko|ja|ふとん太鼓|1=Futon Daiko|12=draft}} - {{ill|Chōsa|ja|頂載|12=draft}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Nada Fighting Festival|ja|灘のけんか祭り|12=draft}} * {{ill|Niihama Taiko Festival|ja|新居浜太鼓祭り|12=draft}} * {{ill|Chōsa.net|ja|ちょうさドットＮＥＴ|12=draft}}
{{Japanese festivals}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Chōsa]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Japanese festivals]] [[Category:Floats and portable shrines]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Taikodai]] [[en:Taikodai]] [[ja:太鼓台]] [[sn:aikodai]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Taikodai}}

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2019年3月}} '''太鼓台'''（たいこだい）とは、 # [[太鼓]]を載せる台。 # [[神社]]の[[祭礼]]において練り出される[[山車]]の一種。内部に[[太鼓]]が積まれており、太鼓に合わせて音頭をとって担ぐ。'''舁き山'''（かきやま、担ぎ山）と'''曳き山'''（ひきやま、引き山）がある。 == 太鼓台（概要） == [[ファイル:甲八幡神社 秋祭り 本宮 甲池に並ぶ屋台.jpg|thumb|甲池に並ぶ神輿屋根屋台（太鼓台）の様子
（[[兵庫県]][[姫路市]][[甲八幡神社]]）]] [[ファイル:Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine Mikoshi.JPG|thumb|太鼓台（屋台）[[宮入り]]の様子
（[[兵庫県]][[三木市]][[大宮八幡宮 (三木市)|大宮八幡宮]]）]] [[画像:Niihamataikomatsuri02.jpg|right|thumb|太鼓台/新居浜太鼓祭り（[[日本三大喧嘩祭り]]）鉢合わせの様子
([[愛媛県]][[新居浜市]])]] [[ファイル:Nakamozu-cho Futon-Daiko 002.webm|thumb|太鼓台（ふとん太鼓）
（[[大阪府]][[堺市]][[北区 (堺市)|北区]]中百舌鳥町）]] 太鼓台は、[[瀬戸内海]]沿岸を中心に[[西日本]]一帯で多く見られる。地域によって様々な違いがあるが、2～6名程度の人間が乗り込んで内部の太鼓を叩き、その太鼓の音に合わせて掛け声をかけ、数十人～百人以上で担ぎ上げ、練り歩くという形式は共通している。他の呼称として'''太鼓'''・'''[[ふとん太鼓]]'''・'''ちょうさ'''・'''頂載（ちょうさい）'''・'''千歳楽（千才楽）'''・'''ヤッサ'''・'''四つ太鼓'''・'''ふとん御輿'''・'''御輿太鼓'''・'''布団だんじり'''・'''太鼓山'''等、総称として'''屋台'''・'''太鼓屋台'''等がある。 神輿が神社の所有物であるのに対して、太鼓台は山車と同様に氏子地域からの出し物であることが多く、太鼓台の祭礼における役割は[[神幸祭|神輿渡御]]のお供や先導（[[露払い]]）、および神前での[[練り]]による[[奉納]]等である。 === 由来（掛け声など） === この太鼓台という名称には、練り歩く際に太鼓の存在がどれほど重要かが現れている。太鼓の叩き方は地方によって微妙に異なるが どの地方でも基本的には「ドン、ドドドン、ドドドン、ドドドン、デンドン（→ドドドンに戻る)」と打ち、掛け声も「''チョーサージャ''（又は''ヨーイッサージャ''）」「''ソウリャ、ソウリャ'' 」「''ヨッサイ、ヨッサイ''」「''ヨーサージャ、ヨイヨイサージャ'' 」「''ベーラーベーラー、ベラッショッショ''」等々地方ごとに特徴がある。中には、地理的に離れた地方同士で類似した掛け声をもつところもある。 龍は雨を呼ぶ神、またはその[[神使|使い]]とされていることから、屋台の上部の重とよばれる布団を締める布団締めの飾り帯を金糸で縫われた龍で覆い、重の四隅の括りは雨雲を、括りの両端から伸びる房は雨をあらわすものとされた。このため、古来より渇水の多い[[香川県]]～[[愛媛県]]東部等での特に稲作が盛んな地域では、神輿としての用途よりもむしろ「'''[[雨乞い]]神具'''」としての性格をもって太鼓台が分布した。 太鼓台には[[神輿]]（神の輿）としての役割はないが、布団屋根の太鼓台の布団部分、また神輿屋根の太鼓台の[[擬宝珠]]部分には神霊が宿ると考える地域があることから、ある種の[[依り代]]とも考えられる。このようなことから、太鼓台に女性が触れることを[[タブー|禁忌]]とする（した）地方も多い。少年が太鼓を叩くところがあり、この場合は[[稚児]]などのように[[厚化粧]]となったり豪華な衣装を着たり、大人に肩車され地面に足を付けない所もある。 曳き山の山車と舁き山の太鼓台との大きく異なる点は、曳き山が引くという形式であるのに対して、舁き山は神輿と同様に担いて運行されることである。担ぐことによって足場状態の影響を受けにくいという面があり、段差や階段等の高低差のある場所や山間部等の坂路の多い地域においても比較的容易に運行することができる。 神前や観衆への見せ場（交差点等の広くなった場所）において最も多く行われる練りは、太鼓台を頭上高く持ち上げて舁く「[[差し上げ]]」である。その他にも、放り上げる、大きく揺らす、回転させる、土台（台輪）部分のみで担ぐ、複数で練り競う（[[かきくらべ]]）、どちらが早く差し上げられるかを競う、等といった動作で練るところがある。これはその地方の伝統が反映された特徴によるもので、その土地の祭礼ごとに異なる。 太鼓台の様式や外観にその地域の土地柄や文化を反映した伝統的な形態を残す地域もあるが、地域経済の発展や氏子主導の祭りへの変化から祭りに[[イベント]]としての要素が強まった地域が増加、見せる祭りとして大型化した太鼓台や飾り幕・刺繍等による豪華な装飾を施したもの、流行の主流となりつつある形態に変更したものが多く見られるようになった。珍しい例であるが地方によってはその地方特有に個別の変化をして太鼓台そのものの外観がすっかり変わってしまったところもある（愛媛県西条市の[[#太鼓台（曳き山）|御輿]]が顕著な例）。 === 基本的な構造 === [[画像:Nadanokenkamatsuri-yatai42.jpg|thumb|150px|屋台内部の太鼓　[[灘のけんか祭り]]]] [[ファイル:大阪 堺 西湊 ふとん太鼓.jpg|thumb|200px|[[大阪府]][[堺市]]の[[ふとん太鼓]] 堺型]] * 台輪と呼ばれる土台部分に太鼓、担ぐための舁き棒（4本が最も多い）、高欄、四本柱と続き、この上に支輪と四方を飾る雲板、そしてその上に布団または重と呼ばれる屋根という構造が一般的である。 * 布団（重）は2～8重と地方ごとの伝統によって段数が異なる。また1段ごとに色が違うもの、1段ごと四隅に小さな房を付けたもの等様々である。 * 太鼓台の屋根にあたる布団部分の四隅には トンボ・まくら・括りと呼ばれる大きな布製の結び飾りがあり、これは雨雲を模したものとされる。 * 重の四方おのおのの側面に布団締めと呼ばれる飾りがあり、古い形態の太鼓台では帯または綱などで文字通りこれで布団を束ねる実用的な部品であった。 * この布団締めは刺繍などの装飾などによって時代とともに豪華さを増していき、近年の太鼓台では金糸で立体に高縫いされた左右一対のつがいの[[竜|龍]]の刺繍が 重の側面のほとんどを覆い隠すまでとなり、重を束ねる帯としての役割から重を飾る飾り面として変化している。 * 近年の太鼓台では複数積み重ねた枠型をもちいて重ねた布団を模したものとなっており、これは近年の大型化にともない軽量化の必要性が生じたためと おもに市街での運行途中に電線や民家の屋根などとの接触を避けるため高所に警護の人員を配置せねばならなくなったことによるもので 近代～現代ならではの変化であるといえる。 * 布団屋根以外の形態としては　神輿屋根や山車型の破風屋根のもの、また布団や屋根のないもの（支輪まで、または高欄までの簡素な構造）がある。 === ふとん太鼓　=== [[ふとん太鼓]]（ふとんだいこ）は大阪府、兵庫県などで担がれる太鼓台のことである。飾り山車の一種であり、形状的な最大の特徴は、正方形の巨大な布団を屋根にあたる部分に逆ピラミッド型に積む点にある。布団だんじり、布団神輿、布団屋台などとも呼ばれる。 [[ふとん太鼓]]を参照 === 神輿屋根屋台 === [[File:甲八幡神社 秋祭り 本宮 練り合わせの様子.jpg|thumb|[[甲八幡神社|甲八幡神社秋祭り]] 神輿屋根同士の練り合わせの様子 2023年撮影 [[兵庫県]][[姫路市]][[甲八幡神社]] ]] {{詳細記事|播州の秋祭り}} 神輿屋根型屋台は神輿と同様に宝形造りの屋根を持つものである。この型の太鼓台は播磨地域以外の太鼓台とは趣を異にしており、播州地域の太鼓台の特徴の一つと言える。総じて呼称は「'''屋台'''」や「'''やっさ'''」であり、装飾や運行形態によって中播方面(灘型神輿屋根屋台)と西播方面(魚吹型神輿屋根屋台)とで2種類に分けられるが、新調等の事情で手放された屋台を別の地域で使用することもあるため、地域によってはこれらの屋台が混ざった祭りもある。また、[[正八幡神社秋祭り]]のようにふとん太鼓と神輿屋根屋台が同じ地域の祭りに参加することもあり、これらは[[神南町|姫路市北部(旧神南町地域)]]や[[福崎町|神崎郡福崎町]]といった中播磨北部で行われている。 奉納する神社によって神輿屋根の紋は大抵決まっており、八幡宮の場合は前後に各大歳神社の紋や龍や虎の彫刻、左右は左巴紋で統一されている。天満宮の場合は四方全てに加賀梅鉢紋を付ける。しかし、天満宮でも前後に大歳神社の紋を付けたり、左巴紋を付ける屋台もある。その他、四方異なる紋を付けている地区もある。 === 千歳楽 === [[ファイル:061029otoshima nakayama.JPG|サムネイル|戸島神社　　中山千歳楽|149x149ピクセル]] [[岡山県]]南西部の秋祭りに繰り出す太鼓台は千歳楽・千才楽・千載楽（せんざいらく・せんだいろく）と呼ばれる。千歳楽は高梁市以南の備中國分で広く分布するが、備讃瀬戸の島嶼部香川県側でも一部この呼称が使われている。備前國分の瀬戸内市牛窓にも同形態の太鼓台が存在するが牛窓での呼称は「どんでんどん」。太鼓台を担ぐ際の担ぎ唄として「千歳楽」を唄うので「千歳楽」の呼称になったとする説がある。 千歳楽は布団を3枚もしくは5枚を載せたモノが一般的であるが、中には2枚布団（倉敷市連島）7枚布団（浅口市旧金光町）のモノもある。総重量は1トン程度までで他地域の太鼓台と比べ総じて小ぶり。 千歳楽が出る祭で最も賑わうのは10月最終土日に行われる倉敷市玉島乙島の戸島神社秋季例大祭（乙島祭り）。千歳楽8台、御船3台、だんじり1台が地区内を巡行する。 千歳楽の唄[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ei_ICXltM_8]を歌いながら練り歩いた後、高く上げて「さした」と叫ぶところもある。 === ちょうさ === [[File:nightchousa.jpg|thumb|[[琴弾八幡宮大祭]]奉納のちょうさ 2018年撮影 [[香川県]][[観音寺市]][[琴弾八幡宮]] 貮號本若太鼓]] 主に[[香川県]][[観音寺市]]で秋季大祭で使用される太鼓台。 布団（重）を7段重ねした「七重」、担ぎ棒の上に「掛布団」と呼ばれる豪華な布団を2枚(主に豊浜・大野原)・4枚(主に琴弾八幡宮)乗せており、200以上の部品で構成されている。 総重量2t、大きなところでは3tを越え、高さは5メートル～5.2メートル位あり、担ぎ棒の長さが13～14メートルで計4本ある。 交差点、広場、神前などの見せ場で太鼓台を頭上高く手で持ち上げる、放り上げる、大きく揺らす、回転などによる奉納が行われる。これらのことを、「[[差し上げ]]」・「[[かきくらべ]]」・「中狂い」という。近年では投光器や派手な電飾でライトアップされる。 === 新居浜型太鼓台 === [[ファイル:Saijou mikoshi.JPG|thumb|upright=0.8|[[西条祭り]]の御輿屋台2006年10月 [[愛媛県]][[西条市]] [[伊曽乃神社]]祭礼]] 四国の[[東予]]・[[西讃]]エリア、特に[[愛媛県]]の[[新居浜太鼓祭り]]や[[西条祭り]]などで見られる大型の太鼓台。豪華な金糸刺繍の飾り幕が施されており、高さ5.5m、重量2.5t（総重量は3t以上）となる。隣接する四国中央市や香川県坂出市・三豊市などにも分布している。 === 福島県の太鼓台 === 東北の[[福島県]][[中通り]]地方に伝わる太鼓台及び連山車は、西日本で見られるものとは異なる。こちらの太鼓台は曳山形式を取っており、また車輪の数は前輪・後輪が2つずつある。舵はある地域とない地域があり、例として[[二本松提灯祭り|二本松の提灯祭り]]の太鼓台には舵がない。屋根は破風屋根で蟇股と欄間に彫刻と金箔を施し、その下の四方に裳階を付ける。内部には前進する方向に締太鼓を3～4個設置、後方に大太鼓が置かれる。 太鼓台の内部では書いて字の通り、太鼓や笛を演奏するようになっている。一部に装飾品の幕を張り、運行する。昼間は前進方向の屋根に町の字紋などを染め抜いた旗を二本、交差させて置く。夜間は屋根に提灯を飾り付けるための枠を設置、300個以上の提灯を飾りつける。 [[二本松市]]の[[二本松神社]][[二本松提灯祭り|秋季例大祭]]を中心に、同市内小浜の塩松神社祭礼（通称・[http://evergreen-net.jp/2018/06/03/%E5%B0%8F%E6%B5%9C%E3%81%AE%E7%B4%8B%E4%BB%98%E7%A5%AD%E3%82%8A/ 小浜の紋付祭り]）や[[本宮市]]の[[安達太良神社]]祭礼、[[郡山市]]の[[安積国造神社秋季例大祭|安積国造神社祭礼]]、[[大玉村]]の玉井神社祭礼など、[[二本松藩|旧二本松藩]]領域に多く見られる。[[福島市]]では[[連山車]]という名前で伝わっており、主に[[福島稲荷神社]]の祭礼にて曳き廻しが行われる。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == {{関連項目過剰|date=2019年3月}} * [[東北]] ** [[二本松提灯祭り]] ** [[北部先囃子]] ** [[南部先囃子]] ** [[連山車]] * [[近畿]] ** [[ふとん太鼓]] *** [[堺まつりふとん太鼓連合保存会]] *** [[友井保存会中南之町ふとん太鼓]] *** 宮前まつり[[宮前ふとん太鼓巡行]] ** [[播州の秋祭り]] *** revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2019年3月}} '''太鼓台'''（たいこだい）とは、 # [[太鼓]]を載せる台。 # [[神社]]の[[祭礼]]において練り出される[[山車]]の一種。内部に[[太鼓]]が積まれており、太鼓に合わせて音頭をとって担ぐ。'''舁き山'''（かきやま、担ぎ山）と'''曳き山'''（ひきやま、引き山）がある。 == 太鼓台（概要） == [[ファイル:甲八幡神社 秋祭り 本宮 甲池に並ぶ屋台.jpg|thumb|甲池に並ぶ神輿屋根屋台（太鼓台）の様子
（[[兵庫県]][[姫路市]][[甲八幡神社]]）]] [[ファイル:Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine Mikoshi.JPG|thumb|太鼓台（屋台）[[宮入り]]の様子
（[[兵庫県]][[三木市]][[大宮八幡宮 (三木市)|大宮八幡宮]]）]] [[画像:Niihamataikomatsuri02.jpg|right|thumb|太鼓台/新居浜太鼓祭り（[[日本三大喧嘩祭り]]）鉢合わせの様子
([[愛媛県]][[新居浜市]])]] [[ファイル:Nakamozu-cho Futon-Daiko 002.webm|thumb|太鼓台（ふとん太鼓）
（[[大阪府]][[堺市]][[北区 (堺市)|北区]]中百舌鳥町）]] 太鼓台は、[[瀬戸内海]]沿岸を中心に[[西日本]]一帯で多く見られる。地域によって様々な違いがあるが、2～6名程度の人間が乗り込んで内部の太鼓を叩き、その太鼓の音に合わせて掛け声をかけ、数十人～百人以上で担ぎ上げ、練り歩くという形式は共通している。他の呼称として'''太鼓'''・'''[[ふとん太鼓]]'''・'''ちょうさ'''・'''頂載（ちょうさい）'''・'''千歳楽（千才楽）'''・'''ヤッサ'''・'''四つ太鼓'''・'''ふとん御輿'''・'''御輿太鼓'''・'''布団だんじり'''・'''太鼓山'''等、総称として'''屋台'''・'''太鼓屋台'''等がある。 神輿が神社の所有物であるのに対して、太鼓台は山車と同様に氏子地域からの出し物であることが多く、太鼓台の祭礼における役割は[[神幸祭|神輿渡御]]のお供や先導（[[露払い]]）、および神前での[[練り]]による[[奉納]]等である。 === 由来（掛け声など） === この太鼓台という名称には、練り歩く際に太鼓の存在がどれほど重要かが現れている。太鼓の叩き方は地方によって微妙に異なるが どの地方でも基本的には「ドン、ドドドン、ドドドン、ドドドン、デンドン（→ドドドンに戻る)」と打ち、掛け声も「''チョーサージャ''（又は''ヨーイッサージャ''）」「''ソウリャ、ソウリャ'' 」「''ヨッサイ、ヨッサイ''」「''ヨーサージャ、ヨイヨイサージャ'' 」「''ベーラーベーラー、ベラッショッショ''」等々地方ごとに特徴がある。中には、地理的に離れた地方同士で類似した掛け声をもつところもある。 龍は雨を呼ぶ神、またはその[[神使|使い]]とされていることから、屋台の上部の重とよばれる布団を締める布団締めの飾り帯を金糸で縫われた龍で覆い、重の四隅の括りは雨雲を、括りの両端から伸びる房は雨をあらわすものとされた。このため、古来より渇水の多い[[香川県]]～[[愛媛県]]東部等での特に稲作が盛んな地域では、神輿としての用途よりもむしろ「'''[[雨乞い]]神具'''」としての性格をもって太鼓台が分布した。 太鼓台には[[神輿]]（神の輿）としての役割はないが、布団屋根の太鼓台の布団部分、また神輿屋根の太鼓台の[[擬宝珠]]部分には神霊が宿ると考える地域があることから、ある種の[[依り代]]とも考えられる。このようなことから、太鼓台に女性が触れることを[[タブー|禁忌]]とする（した）地方も多い。少年が太鼓を叩くところがあり、この場合は[[稚児]]などのように[[厚化粧]]となったり豪華な衣装を着たり、大人に肩車され地面に足を付けない所もある。 曳き山の山車と舁き山の太鼓台との大きく異なる点は、曳き山が引くという形式であるのに対して、舁き山は神輿と同様に担いて運行されることである。担ぐことによって足場状態の影響を受けにくいという面があり、段差や階段等の高低差のある場所や山間部等の坂路の多い地域においても比較的容易に運行することができる。 神前や観衆への見せ場（交差点等の広くなった場所）において最も多く行われる練りは、太鼓台を頭上高く持ち上げて舁く「[[差し上げ]]」である。その他にも、放り上げる、大きく揺らす、回転させる、土台（台輪）部分のみで担ぐ、複数で練り競う（[[かきくらべ]]）、どちらが早く差し上げられるかを競う、等といった動作で練るところがある。これはその地方の伝統が反映された特徴によるもので、その土地の祭礼ごとに異なる。 太鼓台の様式や外観にその地域の土地柄や文化を反映した伝統的な形態を残す地域もあるが、地域経済の発展や氏子主導の祭りへの変化から祭りに[[イベント]]としての要素が強まった地域が増加、見せる祭りとして大型化した太鼓台や飾り幕・刺繍等による豪華な装飾を施したもの、流行の主流となりつつある形態に変更したものが多く見られるようになった。珍しい例であるが地方によってはその地方特有に個別の変化をして太鼓台そのものの外観がすっかり変わってしまったところもある（愛媛県西条市の[[#太鼓台（曳き山）|御輿]]が顕著な例）。 === 基本的な構造 === [[画像:Nadanokenkamatsuri-yatai42.jpg|thumb|150px|屋台内部の太鼓　[[灘のけんか祭り]]]] [[ファイル:大阪 堺 西湊 ふとん太鼓.jpg|thumb|200px|[[大阪府]][[堺市]]の[[ふとん太鼓]] 堺型]] * 台輪と呼ばれる土台部分に太鼓、担ぐための舁き棒（4本が最も多い）、高欄、四本柱と続き、この上に支輪と四方を飾る雲板、そしてその上に布団または重と呼ばれる屋根という構造が一般的である。 * 布団（重）は2～8重と地方ごとの伝統によって段数が異なる。また1段ごとに色が違うもの、1段ごと四隅に小さな房を付けたもの等様々である。 * 太鼓台の屋根にあたる布団部分の四隅には トンボ・まくら・括りと呼ばれる大きな布製の結び飾りがあり、これは雨雲を模したものとされる。 * 重の四方おのおのの側面に布団締めと呼ばれる飾りがあり、古い形態の太鼓台では帯または綱などで文字通りこれで布団を束ねる実用的な部品であった。 * この布団締めは刺繍などの装飾などによって時代とともに豪華さを増していき、近年の太鼓台では金糸で立体に高縫いされた左右一対のつがいの[[竜|龍]]の刺繍が 重の側面のほとんどを覆い隠すまでとなり、重を束ねる帯としての役割から重を飾る飾り面として変化している。 * 近年の太鼓台では複数積み重ねた枠型をもちいて重ねた布団を模したものとなっており、これは近年の大型化にともない軽量化の必要性が生じたためと おもに市街での運行途中に電線や民家の屋根などとの接触を避けるため高所に警護の人員を配置せねばならなくなったことによるもので 近代～現代ならではの変化であるといえる。 * 布団屋根以外の形態としては　神輿屋根や山車型の破風屋根のもの、また布団や屋根のないもの（支輪まで、または高欄までの簡素な構造）がある。 === ふとん太鼓　=== [[ふとん太鼓]]（ふとんだいこ）は大阪府、兵庫県などで担がれる太鼓台のことである。飾り山車の一種であり、形状的な最大の特徴は、正方形の巨大な布団を屋根にあたる部分に逆ピラミッド型に積む点にある。布団だんじり、布団神輿、布団屋台などとも呼ばれる。 [[ふとん太鼓]]を参照 === 神輿屋根屋台 === [[File:甲八幡神社 秋祭り 本宮 練り合わせの様子.jpg|thumb|[[甲八幡神社|甲八幡神社秋祭り]] 神輿屋根同士の練り合わせの様子 2023年撮影 [[兵庫県]][[姫路市]][[甲八幡神社]] ]] {{詳細記事|播州の秋祭り}} 神輿屋根型屋台は神輿と同様に宝形造りの屋根を持つものである。この型の太鼓台は播磨地域以外の太鼓台とは趣を異にしており、播州地域の太鼓台の特徴の一つと言える。総じて呼称は「'''屋台'''」や「'''やっさ'''」であり、装飾や運行形態によって中播方面(灘型神輿屋根屋台)と西播方面(魚吹型神輿屋根屋台)とで2種類に分けられるが、新調等の事情で手放された屋台を別の地域で使用することもあるため、地域によってはこれらの屋台が混ざった祭りもある。また、[[正八幡神社秋祭り]]のようにふとん太鼓と神輿屋根屋台が同じ地域の祭りに参加することもあり、これらは[[神南町|姫路市北部(旧神南町地域)]]や[[福崎町|神崎郡福崎町]]といった中播磨北部で行われている。 奉納する神社によって神輿屋根の紋は大抵決まっており、八幡宮の場合は前後に各大歳神社の紋や龍や虎の彫刻、左右は左巴紋で統一されている。天満宮の場合は四方全てに加賀梅鉢紋を付ける。しかし、天満宮でも前後に大歳神社の紋を付けたり、左巴紋を付ける屋台もある。その他、四方異なる紋を付けている地区もある。 === 千歳楽 === [[ファイル:061029otoshima nakayama.JPG|サムネイル|戸島神社　　中山千歳楽|149x149ピクセル]] [[岡山県]]南西部の秋祭りに繰り出す太鼓台は千歳楽・千才楽・千載楽（せんざいらく・せんだいろく）と呼ばれる。千歳楽は高梁市以南の備中國分で広く分布するが、備讃瀬戸の島嶼部香川県側でも一部この呼称が使われている。備前國分の瀬戸内市牛窓にも同形態の太鼓台が存在するが牛窓での呼称は「どんでんどん」。太鼓台を担ぐ際の担ぎ唄として「千歳楽」を唄うので「千歳楽」の呼称になったとする説がある。 千歳楽は布団を3枚もしくは5枚を載せたモノが一般的であるが、中には2枚布団（倉敷市連島）7枚布団（浅口市旧金光町）のモノもある。総重量は1トン程度までで他地域の太鼓台と比べ総じて小ぶり。 千歳楽が出る祭で最も賑わうのは10月最終土日に行われる倉敷市玉島乙島の戸島神社秋季例大祭（乙島祭り）。千歳楽8台、御船3台、だんじり1台が地区内を巡行する。 千歳楽の唄[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ei_ICXltM_8]を歌いながら練り歩いた後、高く上げて「さした」と叫ぶところもある。 === ちょうさ === [[File:nightchousa.jpg|thumb|[[琴弾八幡宮大祭]]奉納のちょうさ 2018年撮影 [[香川県]][[観音寺市]][[琴弾八幡宮]] 貮號本若太鼓]] 主に[[香川県]][[観音寺市]]で秋季大祭で使用される太鼓台。 布団（重）を7段重ねした「七重」、担ぎ棒の上に「掛布団」と呼ばれる豪華な布団を2枚(主に豊浜・大野原)・4枚(主に琴弾八幡宮)乗せており、200以上の部品で構成されている。 総重量2t、大きなところでは3tを越え、高さは5メートル～5.2メートル位あり、担ぎ棒の長さが13～14メートルで計4本ある。 交差点、広場、神前などの見せ場で太鼓台を頭上高く手で持ち上げる、放り上げる、大きく揺らす、回転などによる奉納が行われる。これらのことを、「[[差し上げ]]」・「[[かきくらべ]]」・「中狂い」という。近年では投光器や派手な電飾でライトアップされる。 === 新居浜型太鼓台 === [[ファイル:Saijou mikoshi.JPG|thumb|upright=0.8|[[西条祭り]]の御輿屋台2006年10月 [[愛媛県]][[西条市]] [[伊曽乃神社]]祭礼]] 四国の[[東予]]・[[西讃]]エリア、特に[[愛媛県]]の[[新居浜太鼓祭り]]や[[西条祭り]]などで見られる大型の太鼓台。豪華な金糸刺繍の飾り幕が施されており、高さ5.5m、重量2.5t（総重量は3t以上）となる。隣接する四国中央市や香川県坂出市・三豊市などにも分布している。 === 福島県の太鼓台 === 東北の[[福島県]][[中通り]]地方に伝わる太鼓台及び連山車は、西日本で見られるものとは異なる。こちらの太鼓台は曳山形式を取っており、また車輪の数は前輪・後輪が2つずつある。舵はある地域とない地域があり、例として[[二本松提灯祭り|二本松の提灯祭り]]の太鼓台には舵がない。屋根は破風屋根で蟇股と欄間に彫刻と金箔を施し、その下の四方に裳階を付ける。内部には前進する方向に締太鼓を3～4個設置、後方に大太鼓が置かれる。 太鼓台の内部では書いて字の通り、太鼓や笛を演奏するようになっている。一部に装飾品の幕を張り、運行する。昼間は前進方向の屋根に町の字紋などを染め抜いた旗を二本、交差させて置く。夜間は屋根に提灯を飾り付けるための枠を設置、300個以上の提灯を飾りつける。 [[二本松市]]の[[二本松神社]][[二本松提灯祭り|秋季例大祭]]を中心に、同市内小浜の塩松神社祭礼（通称・[http://evergreen-net.jp/2018/06/03/%E5%B0%8F%E6%B5%9C%E3%81%AE%E7%B4%8B%E4%BB%98%E7%A5%AD%E3%82%8A/ 小浜の紋付祭り]）や[[本宮市]]の[[安達太良神社]]祭礼、[[郡山市]]の[[安積国造神社秋季例大祭|安積国造神社祭礼]]、[[大玉村]]の玉井神社祭礼など、[[二本松藩|旧二本松藩]]領域に多く見られる。[[福島市]]では[[連山車]]という名前で伝わっており、主に[[福島稲荷神社]]の祭礼にて曳き廻しが行われる。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 [http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/page/dir002875.html 二本松の提灯祭り] {{デフォルトソート:たいこたい}} [[Category:日本の祭り]] [[Category:山車・屋台]]

{{Citation needed|date=March 2019}} '''Taikodai''' (太鼓台) refers to:
1. A platform used to place a {{ill|taiko|ja|太鼓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} drum. 2. A type of {{ill|festival float|ja|山車|qq=draft|qq=draft}} used in {{ill|shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} festivals. It typically houses a drum inside and is carried or pulled in accordance with the rhythm of the drum. There are two main types: '''Kakiyama''' (舁き山, carried floats) and '''Hikiyama''' (曳き山, pulled floats).
== Overview of Taikodai == [[File:甲八幡神社 秋祭り 本宮 甲池に並ぶ屋台.jpg|thumb|View of portable shrine roof floats (taikodai) lined up at {{ill|Kōhachiman Shrine|ja|甲八幡神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in [[Himeji]], Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[File:Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine Mikoshi.JPG|thumb|Taikodai ({{ill|portable shrine entry|ja|宮入り|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) at {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in [[Miki]], Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[File:Niihamataikomatsuri02.jpg|thumb|Taikodai at the {{ill|Niihama Taiko Festival|ja|新居浜太鼓祭り|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (part of the {{ill|Three Great Fighting Festivals|ja|日本三大喧嘩祭り|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) in {{ill|Niihama|ja|新居浜市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Ehime Prefecture]] [[File:Nakamozu-cho Futon-Daiko 002.webm|thumb|Taikodai (Futon Daiko) in [[Nakamozu]], [[Sakai]], Osaka Prefecture]]
Taikodai are especially common along the {{ill|Seto Inland Sea|ja|瀬戸内海|qq=draft|qq=draft}} region and throughout western Japan. While their specific designs vary by region, the general practice involves 2–6 people entering the float to play the drum inside, while tens to over a hundred people carry it, shouting rhythmic chants as they parade. alternate_name include '''Taiko''', '''Futon Taiko''', '''Chōsa''', '''Chōsai''' (頂載), '''Senzairaku''' (千歳楽), '''Yassa''', '''Yotsutaiko''' (四つ太鼓), '''Futon Mikoshi''' (布団御輿), '''Mikoshi Taiko''', '''Futon Dashi''' (布団だんじり), '''Taiko Yama''' (太鼓山), and others. As a collective term, they are also called '''Yatai''' (屋台) or '''Taiko Yatai''' (太鼓屋台).
Unlike {{ill|mikoshi|ja|神輿|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which are owned by the shrine, taikodai are usually provided by the community and serve to escort or lead the mikoshi during a {{ill|Shinkōsai|ja|神幸祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, as well as to offer performances before the shrine as an act of {{ill|devotion|ja|奉納|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
=== Origins and Chants === The name "taikodai" highlights the drum's central role in the procession. The drumming pattern and accompanying chants vary by region but often follow rhythmic patterns such as "Don, Dododon, Dododon, Dododon, Dendon (and repeat)." Common chants include: - "Chōsaja" (or "Yoissaja") - "Sōrya, Sōrya" - "Yossai, Yossai" - "Yōsaja, Yoiyoisaja" - "Bērabēra, Berasshossho"
The dragon, symbolizing rain-bringing deities or messengers of the gods, is often featured in taikodai decoration. The fabric straps used to secure the "futon" (mattress-like tiers) atop the float are embroidered with golden dragons, and tassels on the straps symbolize rain. Thus, in regions like {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|ja|香川県|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and eastern {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|ja|愛媛県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, taikodai have historically been associated more with rainmaking rituals than with mikoshi.
While taikodai do not carry divine spirits like mikoshi, some regions consider the futon or roof portions as sacred vessels where spirits may reside. For this reason, women were traditionally prohibited from touching taikodai in certain areas. Boys who play the drums inside may dress elaborately and avoid touching the ground, much like traditional {{ill|Chigo|ja|稚児|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Taikodai are distinct from hikiyama floats in that they are carried rather than pulled. This makes them easier to maneuver in mountainous areas or over uneven terrain. They are often raised high overhead in displays known as "sashiage" (差し上げ) and may also be shaken, rotated, or competed in lifting contests.
== Structure == [[File:Nadanokenkamatsuri-yatai42.jpg|thumb|150px|Interior drum of a taikodai at the {{ill|Nada Fighting Festival|ja|灘のけんか祭り|qq=draft|qq=draft}}]] [[File:大阪 堺 西湊 ふとん太鼓.jpg|thumb|200px|Futon Taiko from {{ill|Sakai|ja|堺市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Osaka Prefecture]]
- The base, called the "taiwa" (台輪), supports the drum, carrying poles, railings, and vertical pillars, with a roof of futon or heavy tiers. - The futon (重) tiers vary from 2 to 8 in number, depending on regional tradition. - Ornamental features such as tassels, decorative straps, and embroidered dragons are common. - Modern taikodai may use lightweight materials for ease of transport.
For further details on specific styles, see: - {{ill|Futon Taiko|ja|ふとん太鼓|1=Futon Daiko|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - {{ill|Chōsa|ja|頂載|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Nada Fighting Festival|ja|灘のけんか祭り|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Niihama Taiko Festival|ja|新居浜太鼓祭り|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Chōsa.net|ja|ちょうさドットＮＥＴ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
{{Japanese festivals}}

[[sn:Taikodai]] [[en:Taikodai]] [[ja:太鼓台]] [[sn:aikodai]]

It typically houses a drum inside and is carried or pulled in accordance with the rhythm of the drum. There are two main types: '''Kakiyama''' (舁き山, carried floats) and '''Hikiyama''' (曳き山, pulled floats).
== Overview of Taikodai == [[File:甲八幡神社 秋祭り 本宮 甲池に並ぶ屋台.jpg|thumb|View of portable shrine roof floats (taikodai) lined up at {{ill|Kōhachiman Shrine|ja|甲八幡神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōhachiman Shrine|qq=|1=Kabuto-hachiman Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q127427418}} in [[:en:Himeji|Himeji]], Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[File:Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine Mikoshi.JPG|thumb|Taikodai ({{ill|portable shrine entry|ja|宮入り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/portable shrine entry|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神幸祭|1=shinkō-sai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123907414}}) at [[:en:Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Hyōgo)|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki)]] in [[:en:Miki|Miki]], Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[File:Niihamataikomatsuri02.jpg|thumb|Taikodai at the {{ill|Niihama Taiko Festival|ja|新居浜太鼓祭り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niihama Taiko Festival|qq=|1=Niihama Taiko Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501940}} (part of the {{ill|Three Great Fighting Festivals|ja|日本三大喧嘩祭り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Great Fighting Festivals|qq=|1=Three Great Fighting Festivals|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17211680}}) in [[:en:Niihama|Niihama]], Ehime Prefecture]] [[File:Nakamozu-cho Futon-Daiko 002.webm|thumb|Taikodai (Futon Daiko) in [[Nakamozu]], [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]], Osaka Prefecture]]

Unlike [[:en:Mikoshi|mikoshi]], which are owned by the shrine, taikodai are usually provided by the community and serve to escort or lead the mikoshi during a [[:en:Shinko-shiki|Shinkōsai]], as well as to offer performances before the shrine as an act of [[:en:Votive offering|devotion]].
=== Origins and Chants === The name "taikodai" highlights the drum's central role in the procession. The drumming pattern and accompanying chants vary by region but often follow rhythmic patterns such as "Don, Dododon, Dododon, Dododon, Dendon (and repeat)." Common chants include: - "Chōsaja" (or "Yoissaja") - "Sōrya, Sōrya" - "Yossai, Yossai" - "Yōsaja, Yoiyoisaja" - "Bērabēra, Berasshossho"
The dragon, symbolizing rain-bringing deities or messengers of the gods, is often featured in taikodai decoration. The fabric straps used to secure the "futon" (mattress-like tiers) atop the float are embroidered with golden dragons, and tassels on the straps symbolize rain. Thus, in regions like [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]] and eastern [[:en:Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]], taikodai have historically been associated more with rainmaking rituals than with mikoshi.
While taikodai do not carry divine spirits like mikoshi, some regions consider the futon or roof portions as sacred vessels where spirits may reside. For this reason, women were traditionally prohibited from touching taikodai in certain areas. Boys who play the drums inside may dress elaborately and avoid touching the ground, much like traditional {{ill|Chigo|ja|稚児|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chigo|qq=}}.
Taikodai are distinct from hikiyama floats in that they are carried rather than pulled. This makes them easier to maneuver in mountainous areas or over uneven terrain. They are often raised high overhead in displays known as "sashiage" (差し上げ) and may also be shaken, rotated, or competed in lifting contests.

For further details on specific styles, see: - {{ill|Futon Taiko|ja|ふとん太鼓|1=Futon Daiko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futon Taiko|13=User:Immanuelle/Futon Daiko|qq=}} - {{ill|Taikodai|lt=Taikodai|lt=Chōsa|ja|頂載|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōsa|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

They are often raised high overhead in displays known as "sashiage" (差し上げ) and may also be shaken, rotated, or competed in lifting contests.
== Structure == [[File:Nadanokenkamatsuri-yatai42.jpg|thumb|150px|Interior drum of a taikodai at the {{ill|Nada Fighting Festival|ja|灘のけんか祭り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nada Fighting Festival|qq=|1=Nada no kenka matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566794}}]] [[File:大阪 堺 西湊 ふとん太鼓.jpg|thumb|200px|Futon Taiko from [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]], Osaka Prefecture]]
- The base, called the "taiwa" (台輪), supports the drum, carrying poles, railings, and vertical pillars, with a roof of futon or heavy tiers. - The futon (重) tiers vary from 2 to 8 in number, depending on regional tradition. - Ornamental features such as tassels, decorative straps, and embroidered dragons are common. - Modern taikodai may use lightweight materials for ease of transport.
For further details on specific styles, see: - {{ill|Futon Taiko|ja|ふとん太鼓|1=Futon Daiko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futon Taiko|13=User:Immanuelle/Futon Daiko|qq=}} - {{ill|Taikodai|lt=Taikodai|lt=Chōsa|ja|頂載|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōsa|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Nada Fighting Festival|ja|灘のけんか祭り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nada Fighting Festival|qq=|1=Nada no kenka matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566794}} * {{ill|Niihama Taiko Festival|ja|新居浜太鼓祭り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niihama Taiko Festival|qq=|1=Niihama Taiko Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501940}} * {{ill|Chōsa.net|ja|ちょうさドットＮＥＴ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōsa.net|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

[[Category:Parade floats of Japan]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11444734}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太鼓台]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11444734]] Wikidata name: taikodai [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=March 2019}} '''Taikodai''' (太鼓台) refers to:
1. A platform used to place a [[:en:Drum|taiko]] drum. 2. A type of [[:en:Matsuri float|festival float]] used in [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] festivals. It typically houses a drum inside and is carried or pulled in accordance with the rhythm of the drum. There are two main types: '''Kakiyama''' (舁き山, carried floats) and '''Hikiyama''' (曳き山, pulled floats).
== Overview of Taikodai == [[File:甲八幡神社 秋祭り 本宮 甲池に並ぶ屋台.jpg|thumb|View of portable shrine roof floats (taikodai) lined up at {{ill|Kōhachiman Shrine|ja|甲八幡神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōhachiman Shrine|1=Kabuto-hachiman Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q127427418}} in [[:en:Himeji|Himeji]], Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[File:Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine Mikoshi.JPG|thumb|Taikodai ({{ill|portable shrine entry|ja|宮入り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/portable shrine entry|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神幸祭|1=shinkō-sai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123907414}}) at [[:en:Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Hyōgo)|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki)]] in [[:en:Miki|Miki]], Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[File:Niihamataikomatsuri02.jpg|thumb|Taikodai at the {{ill|Niihama Taiko Festival|ja|新居浜太鼓祭り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niihama Taiko Festival|1=Niihama Taiko Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501940}} (part of the {{ill|Three Great Fighting Festivals|ja|日本三大喧嘩祭り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Great Fighting Festivals|1=Three Great Fighting Festivals|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17211680}}) in [[:en:Niihama|Niihama]], Ehime Prefecture]] [[File:Nakamozu-cho Futon-Daiko 002.webm|thumb|Taikodai (Futon Daiko) in [[Nakamozu]], [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]], Osaka Prefecture]]
Taikodai are especially common along the [[:en:Seto Inland Sea|Seto Inland Sea]] region and throughout western Japan. While their specific designs vary by region, the general practice involves 2–6 people entering the float to play the drum inside, while tens to over a hundred people carry it, shouting rhythmic chants as they parade. alternate_name include '''Taiko''', '''Futon Taiko''', '''Chōsa''', '''Chōsai''' (頂載), '''Senzairaku''' (千歳楽), '''Yassa''', '''Yotsutaiko''' (四つ太鼓), '''Futon Mikoshi''' (布団御輿), '''Mikoshi Taiko''', '''Futon Dashi''' (布団だんじり), '''Taiko Yama''' (太鼓山), and others. As a collective term, they are also called '''Yatai''' (屋台) or '''Taiko Yatai''' (太鼓屋台).
Unlike [[:en:Mikoshi|mikoshi]], which are owned by the shrine, taikodai are usually provided by the community and serve to escort or lead the mikoshi during a [[:en:Shinko-shiki|Shinkōsai]], as well as to offer performances before the shrine as an act of [[:en:Votive offering|devotion]].
=== Origins and Chants === The name "taikodai" highlights the drum's central role in the procession. The drumming pattern and accompanying chants vary by region but often follow rhythmic patterns such as "Don, Dododon, Dododon, Dododon, Dendon (and repeat)." Common chants include: - "Chōsaja" (or "Yoissaja") - "Sōrya, Sōrya" - "Yossai, Yossai" - "Yōsaja, Yoiyoisaja" - "Bērabēra, Berasshossho"
The dragon, symbolizing rain-bringing deities or messengers of the gods, is often featured in taikodai decoration. The fabric straps used to secure the "futon" (mattress-like tiers) atop the float are embroidered with golden dragons, and tassels on the straps symbolize rain. Thus, in regions like [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]] and eastern [[:en:Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]], taikodai have historically been associated more with rainmaking rituals than with mikoshi.
While taikodai do not carry divine spirits like mikoshi, some regions consider the futon or roof portions as sacred vessels where spirits may reside. For this reason, women were traditionally prohibited from touching taikodai in certain areas. Boys who play the drums inside may dress elaborately and avoid touching the ground, much like traditional {{ill|Chigo|ja|稚児|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chigo|qq=}}.
Taikodai are distinct from hikiyama floats in that they are carried rather than pulled. This makes them easier to maneuver in mountainous areas or over uneven terrain. They are often raised high overhead in displays known as "sashiage" (差し上げ) and may also be shaken, rotated, or competed in lifting contests.
== Structure == [[File:Nadanokenkamatsuri-yatai42.jpg|thumb|150px|Interior drum of a taikodai at the {{ill|Nada Fighting Festival|ja|灘のけんか祭り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nada Fighting Festival|1=Nada no kenka matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566794}}]] [[File:大阪 堺 西湊 ふとん太鼓.jpg|thumb|200px|Futon Taiko from [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]], Osaka Prefecture]]
- The base, called the "taiwa" (台輪), supports the drum, carrying poles, railings, and vertical pillars, with a roof of futon or heavy tiers. - The futon (重) tiers vary from 2 to 8 in number, depending on regional tradition. - Ornamental features such as tassels, decorative straps, and embroidered dragons are common. - Modern taikodai may use lightweight materials for ease of transport.
For further details on specific styles, see: - {{ill|Futon Taiko|ja|ふとん太鼓|1=Futon Daiko|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futon Taiko|13=User:Immanuelle/Futon Daiko|qq=}} - {{ill|Taikodai|lt=Taikodai|lt=Chōsa|ja|頂載|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōsa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Nada Fighting Festival|ja|灘のけんか祭り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nada Fighting Festival|1=Nada no kenka matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566794}} * {{ill|Niihama Taiko Festival|ja|新居浜太鼓祭り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niihama Taiko Festival|1=Niihama Taiko Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501940}} * {{ill|Chōsa.net|ja|ちょうさドットＮＥＴ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōsa.net|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

It typically houses a drum inside and is carried or pulled in accordance with the rhythm of the drum. There are two main types: '''Kakiyama''' (舁き山, carried floats) and '''Hikiyama''' (曳き山, pulled floats).
== Overview of Taikodai == [[File:甲八幡神社 秋祭り 本宮 甲池に並ぶ屋台.jpg|thumb|View of portable shrine roof floats (taikodai) lined up at {{ill|Kōhachiman Shrine|ja|甲八幡神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōhachiman Shrine|1=Kabuto-hachiman Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q127427418}} in [[:en:Himeji|Himeji]], Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[File:Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine Mikoshi.JPG|thumb|Taikodai ({{ill|portable shrine entry|ja|宮入り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/portable shrine entry|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神幸祭|1=shinkō-sai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123907414}}) at [[:en:Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Hyōgo)|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki)]] in [[:en:Miki|Miki]], Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[File:Niihamataikomatsuri02.jpg|thumb|Taikodai at the {{ill|Niihama Taiko Festival|ja|新居浜太鼓祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niihama Taiko Festival|1=Niihama Taiko Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501940}} (part of the {{ill|Three Great Fighting Festivals|ja|日本三大喧嘩祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Great Fighting Festivals|1=Three Great Fighting Festivals|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17211680}}) in [[:en:Niihama|Niihama]], Ehime Prefecture]] [[File:Nakamozu-cho Futon-Daiko 002.webm|thumb|Taikodai (Futon Daiko) in [[Nakamozu]], [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]], Osaka Prefecture]]
Taikodai are especially common along the [[:en:Seto Inland Sea|Seto Inland Sea]] region and throughout western Japan. While their specific designs vary by region, the general practice involves 2–6 people entering the float to play the drum inside, while tens to over a hundred people carry it, shouting rhythmic chants as they parade. alternate_name include '''Taiko''', '''Futon Taiko''', '''Chōsa''', '''Chōsai''' (頂載), '''Senzairaku''' (千歳楽), '''Yassa''', '''Yotsutaiko''' (四つ太鼓), '''Futon Mikoshi''' (布団御輿), '''Mikoshi Taiko''', '''Futon Dashi''' (布団だんじり), '''Taiko Yama''' (太鼓山), and others. As a collective term, they are also called '''Yatai''' (屋台) or '''Taiko Yatai''' (太鼓屋台).
Unlike [[:en:Mikoshi|mikoshi]], which are owned by the shrine, taikodai are usually provided by the community and serve to escort or lead the mikoshi during a [[:en:Shinko-shiki|Shinkōsai]], as well as to offer performances before the shrine as an act of [[:en:Votive offering|devotion]].
=== Origins and Chants === The name "taikodai" highlights the drum's central role in the procession. The drumming pattern and accompanying chants vary by region but often follow rhythmic patterns such as "Don, Dododon, Dododon, Dododon, Dendon (and repeat)." Common chants include: - "Chōsaja" (or "Yoissaja") - "Sōrya, Sōrya" - "Yossai, Yossai" - "Yōsaja, Yoiyoisaja" - "Bērabēra, Berasshossho"
The dragon, symbolizing rain-bringing deities or messengers of the gods, is often featured in taikodai decoration. The fabric straps used to secure the "futon" (mattress-like tiers) atop the float are embroidered with golden dragons, and tassels on the straps symbolize rain. Thus, in regions like [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]] and eastern [[:en:Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]], taikodai have historically been associated more with rainmaking rituals than with mikoshi.
While taikodai do not carry divine spirits like mikoshi, some regions consider the futon or roof portions as sacred vessels where spirits may reside. For this reason, women were traditionally prohibited from touching taikodai in certain areas. Boys who play the drums inside may dress elaborately and avoid touching the ground, much like traditional {{ill|Chigo|ja|稚児|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chigo|qq=}}.
Taikodai are distinct from hikiyama floats in that they are carried rather than pulled. This makes them easier to maneuver in mountainous areas or over uneven terrain. They are often raised high overhead in displays known as "sashiage" (差し上げ) and may also be shaken, rotated, or competed in lifting contests.
== Structure == [[File:Nadanokenkamatsuri-yatai42.jpg|thumb|150px|Interior drum of a taikodai at the {{ill|Nada Fighting Festival|ja|灘のけんか祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nada Fighting Festival|1=Nada no kenka matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566794}}]] [[File:大阪 堺 西湊 ふとん太鼓.jpg|thumb|200px|Futon Taiko from [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]], Osaka Prefecture]]
- The base, called the "taiwa" (台輪), supports the drum, carrying poles, railings, and vertical pillars, with a roof of futon or heavy tiers. - The futon (重) tiers vary from 2 to 8 in number, depending on regional tradition. - Ornamental features such as tassels, decorative straps, and embroidered dragons are common. - Modern taikodai may use lightweight materials for ease of transport.
For further details on specific styles, see: - {{ill|Futon Taiko|ja|ふとん太鼓|1=Futon Daiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futon Taiko|13=User:Immanuelle/Futon Daiko|qq=}} - {{ill|Taikodai|lt=Taikodai|lt=Chōsa|ja|頂載|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōsa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Nada Fighting Festival|ja|灘のけんか祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nada Fighting Festival|1=Nada no kenka matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566794}} * {{ill|Niihama Taiko Festival|ja|新居浜太鼓祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niihama Taiko Festival|1=Niihama Taiko Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501940}} * {{ill|Chōsa.net|ja|ちょうさドットＮＥＴ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōsa.net|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

The drumming pattern and accompanying chants vary by region but often follow rhythmic patterns such as "Don, Dododon, Dododon, Dododon, Dendon (and repeat)." Common chants include: - "Chōsaja" (or "Yoissaja") - "Sōrya, Sōrya" - "Yossai, Yossai" - "Yōsaja, Yoiyoisaja" - "Bērabēra, Berasshossho"
The dragon, symbolizing rain-bringing deities or messengers of the gods, is often featured in taikodai decoration. The fabric straps used to secure the "futon" (mattress-like tiers) atop the float are embroidered with golden dragons, and tassels on the straps symbolize rain. Thus, in regions like [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]] and eastern [[:en:Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]], taikodai have historically been associated more with rainmaking rituals than with mikoshi.
While taikodai do not carry divine spirits like mikoshi, some regions consider the futon or roof portions as sacred vessels where spirits may reside. For this reason, women were traditionally prohibited from touching taikodai in certain areas. Boys who play the drums inside may dress elaborately and avoid touching the ground, much like traditional {{ill|Chigo|ja|稚児|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chigo|qq=}}.
Taikodai are distinct from hikiyama floats in that they are carried rather than pulled. This makes them easier to maneuver in mountainous areas or over uneven terrain. They are often raised high overhead in displays known as "sashiage" (差し上げ) and may also be shaken, rotated, or competed in lifting contests.

- The base, called the "taiwa" (台輪), supports the drum, carrying poles, railings, and vertical pillars, with a roof of futon or heavy tiers. - The futon (重) tiers vary from 2 to 8 in number, depending on regional tradition. - Ornamental features such as tassels, decorative straps, and embroidered dragons are common. - Modern taikodai may use lightweight materials for ease of transport.

The dragon, symbolizing rain-bringing deities or messengers of the gods, is often featured in taikodai decoration. The fabric straps used to secure the "futon" (mattress-like tiers) atop the float are embroidered with golden dragons, and tassels on the straps symbolize rain. Thus, in regions like [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]] and eastern [[:en:Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]], taikodai have historically been associated more with rainmaking rituals than with mikoshi.
While taikodai do not carry divine spirits like mikoshi, some regions consider the futon or roof portions as sacred vessels where spirits may reside. For this reason, women were traditionally prohibited from touching taikodai in certain areas. Boys who play the drums inside may dress elaborately and avoid touching the ground, much like traditional {{ill|Chigo|ja|稚児|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chigo|qq=}}.
Taikodai are distinct from hikiyama floats in that they are carried rather than pulled. This makes them easier to maneuver in mountainous areas or over uneven terrain. They are often raised high overhead in displays known as "sashiage" (差し上げ) and may also be shaken, rotated, or competed in lifting contests.
== Structure == [[File:Nadanokenkamatsuri-yatai42.jpg|thumb|150px|Interior drum of a taikodai at the {{ill|Nada Fighting Festival|ja|灘のけんか祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nada Fighting Festival|1=Nada no kenka matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566794}}]] [[File:大阪 堺 西湊 ふとん太鼓.jpg|thumb|200px|Futon Taiko from [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]], Osaka Prefecture]]
- The base, called the "taiwa" (台輪), supports the drum, carrying poles, railings, and vertical pillars, with a roof of futon or heavy tiers. - The futon (重) tiers vary from 2 to 8 in number, depending on regional tradition. - Ornamental features such as tassels, decorative straps, and embroidered dragons are common. - Modern taikodai may use lightweight materials for ease of transport.
For further details on specific styles, see: - {{ill|Futon Taiko|ja|ふとん太鼓|1=Futon Daiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futon Taiko|13=User:Immanuelle/Futon Daiko|qq=}} - {{ill|Taikodai|lt=Taikodai|lt=Chōsa|ja|頂載|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōsa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Nada Fighting Festival|ja|灘のけんか祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nada Fighting Festival|1=Nada no kenka matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566794}} * {{ill|Niihama Taiko Festival|ja|新居浜太鼓祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niihama Taiko Festival|1=Niihama Taiko Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501940}} * {{ill|Chōsa.net|ja|ちょうさドットＮＥＴ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōsa.net|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

[[Category:Parade floats of Japan]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11444734}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太鼓台]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11444734]] Wikidata name: taikodai [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=March 2019}} '''Taikodai''' (太鼓台) refers to:
1. A platform used to place a [[:en:Drum|taiko]] drum. 2. A type of [[:en:Matsuri float|festival float]] used in [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] festivals. It typically houses a drum inside and is carried or pulled in accordance with the rhythm of the drum. There are two main types: '''Kakiyama''' (舁き山, carried floats) and '''Hikiyama''' (曳き山, pulled floats).
== Overview of Taikodai == [[File:甲八幡神社 秋祭り 本宮 甲池に並ぶ屋台.jpg|thumb|View of portable shrine roof floats (taikodai) lined up at {{ill|Kōhachiman Shrine|ja|甲八幡神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōhachiman Shrine|1=Kabuto-hachiman Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q127427418}} in [[:en:Himeji|Himeji]], Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[File:Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine Mikoshi.JPG|thumb|Taikodai ({{ill|portable shrine entry|ja|宮入り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/portable shrine entry|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神幸祭|1=shinkō-sai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123907414}}) at [[:en:Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Hyōgo)|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki)]] in [[:en:Miki|Miki]], Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[File:Niihamataikomatsuri02.jpg|thumb|Taikodai at the {{ill|Niihama Taiko Festival|ja|新居浜太鼓祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niihama Taiko Festival|1=Niihama Taiko Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501940}} (part of the {{ill|Three Great Fighting Festivals|ja|日本三大喧嘩祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Great Fighting Festivals|1=Three Great Fighting Festivals|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17211680}}) in [[:en:Niihama|Niihama]], Ehime Prefecture]] [[File:Nakamozu-cho Futon-Daiko 002.webm|thumb|Taikodai (Futon Daiko) in [[Nakamozu]], [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]], Osaka Prefecture]]
Taikodai are especially common along the [[:en:Seto Inland Sea|Seto Inland Sea]] region and throughout western Japan. While their specific designs vary by region, the general practice involves 2–6 people entering the float to play the drum inside, while tens to over a hundred people carry it, shouting rhythmic chants as they parade. alternate_name include '''Taiko''', '''Futon Taiko''', '''Chōsa''', '''Chōsai''' (頂載), '''Senzairaku''' (千歳楽), '''Yassa''', '''Yotsutaiko''' (四つ太鼓), '''Futon Mikoshi''' (布団御輿), '''Mikoshi Taiko''', '''Futon Dashi''' (布団だんじり), '''Taiko Yama''' (太鼓山), and others. As a collective term, they are also called '''Yatai''' (屋台) or '''Taiko Yatai''' (太鼓屋台).
Unlike [[:en:Mikoshi|mikoshi]], which are owned by the shrine, taikodai are usually provided by the community and serve to escort or lead the mikoshi during a [[:en:Shinko-shiki|Shinkōsai]], as well as to offer performances before the shrine as an act of [[:en:Votive offering|devotion]].
=== Origins and Chants === The name "taikodai" highlights the drum's central role in the procession. The drumming pattern and accompanying chants vary by region but often follow rhythmic patterns such as "Don, Dododon, Dododon, Dododon, Dendon (and repeat)." Common chants include: - "Chōsaja" (or "Yoissaja") - "Sōrya, Sōrya" - "Yossai, Yossai" - "Yōsaja, Yoiyoisaja" - "Bērabēra, Berasshossho"
The dragon, symbolizing rain-bringing deities or messengers of the gods, is often featured in taikodai decoration. The fabric straps used to secure the "futon" (mattress-like tiers) atop the float are embroidered with golden dragons, and tassels on the straps symbolize rain. Thus, in regions like [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]] and eastern [[:en:Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]], taikodai have historically been associated more with rainmaking rituals than with mikoshi.
While taikodai do not carry divine spirits like mikoshi, some regions consider the futon or roof portions as sacred vessels where spirits may reside. For this reason, women were traditionally prohibited from touching taikodai in certain areas. Boys who play the drums inside may dress elaborately and avoid touching the ground, much like traditional {{ill|Chigo|ja|稚児|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chigo|qq=}}.
Taikodai are distinct from hikiyama floats in that they are carried rather than pulled. This makes them easier to maneuver in mountainous areas or over uneven terrain. They are often raised high overhead in displays known as "sashiage" (差し上げ) and may also be shaken, rotated, or competed in lifting contests.
== Structure == [[File:Nadanokenkamatsuri-yatai42.jpg|thumb|150px|Interior drum of a taikodai at the {{ill|Nada Fighting Festival|ja|灘のけんか祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nada Fighting Festival|1=Nada no kenka matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566794}}]] [[File:大阪 堺 西湊 ふとん太鼓.jpg|thumb|200px|Futon Taiko from [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]], Osaka Prefecture]]
- The base, called the "taiwa" (台輪), supports the drum, carrying poles, railings, and vertical pillars, with a roof of futon or heavy tiers. - The futon (重) tiers vary from 2 to 8 in number, depending on regional tradition. - Ornamental features such as tassels, decorative straps, and embroidered dragons are common. - Modern taikodai may use lightweight materials for ease of transport.
For further details on specific styles, see: - {{ill|Futon Taiko|ja|ふとん太鼓|1=Futon Daiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futon Taiko|13=User:Immanuelle/Futon Daiko|qq=}} - {{ill|Taikodai|lt=Taikodai|lt=Chōsa|ja|頂載|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōsa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Nada Fighting Festival|ja|灘のけんか祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nada Fighting Festival|1=Nada no kenka matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566794}} * {{ill|Niihama Taiko Festival|ja|新居浜太鼓祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niihama Taiko Festival|1=Niihama Taiko Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501940}} * {{ill|Chōsa.net|ja|ちょうさドットＮＥＴ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōsa.net|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太鼓台]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太鼓台]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11444734]] Wikidata name: taikodai [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=March 2019}} '''Taikodai''' (太鼓台) refers to:
1. A platform used to place a [[:en:Drum|taiko]] drum. 2. A type of [[:en:Matsuri float|festival float]] used in [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] festivals. It typically houses a drum inside and is carried or pulled in accordance with the rhythm of the drum. There are two main types: '''Kakiyama''' (舁き山, carried floats) and '''Hikiyama''' (曳き山, pulled floats).
== Overview of Taikodai == [[File:甲八幡神社 秋祭り 本宮 甲池に並ぶ屋台.jpg|thumb|View of portable shrine roof floats (taikodai) lined up at {{ill|Kōhachiman Shrine|ja|甲八幡神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōhachiman Shrine|1=Kabuto-hachiman Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q127427418}} in [[:en:Himeji|Himeji]], Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[File:Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine Mikoshi.JPG|thumb|Taikodai ({{ill|portable shrine entry|ja|宮入り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/portable shrine entry|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神幸祭|1=shinkō-sai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123907414}}) at [[:en:Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Hyōgo)|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki)]] in [[:en:Miki|Miki]], Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[File:Niihamataikomatsuri02.jpg|thumb|Taikodai at the {{ill|Niihama Taiko Festival|ja|新居浜太鼓祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niihama Taiko Festival|1=Niihama Taiko Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501940}} (part of the {{ill|Three Great Fighting Festivals|ja|日本三大喧嘩祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Great Fighting Festivals|1=Three Great Fighting Festivals|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17211680}}) in [[:en:Niihama|Niihama]], Ehime Prefecture]] [[File:Nakamozu-cho Futon-Daiko 002.webm|thumb|Taikodai (Futon Daiko) in [[Nakamozu]], [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]], Osaka Prefecture]]
Taikodai are especially common along the [[:en:Seto Inland Sea|Seto Inland Sea]] region and throughout western Japan. While their specific designs vary by region, the general practice involves 2–6 people entering the float to play the drum inside, while tens to over a hundred people carry it, shouting rhythmic chants as they parade. alternate_name include '''Taiko''', '''Futon Taiko''', '''Chōsa''', '''Chōsai''' (頂載), '''Senzairaku''' (千歳楽), '''Yassa''', '''Yotsutaiko''' (四つ太鼓), '''Futon Mikoshi''' (布団御輿), '''Mikoshi Taiko''', '''Futon Dashi''' (布団だんじり), '''Taiko Yama''' (太鼓山), and others. As a collective term, they are also called '''Yatai''' (屋台) or '''Taiko Yatai''' (太鼓屋台).
Unlike [[:en:Mikoshi|mikoshi]], which are owned by the shrine, taikodai are usually provided by the community and serve to escort or lead the mikoshi during a [[:en:Shinko-shiki|Shinkōsai]], as well as to offer performances before the shrine as an act of [[:en:Votive offering|devotion]].
=== Origins and Chants === The name "taikodai" highlights the drum's central role in the procession. The drumming pattern and accompanying chants vary by region but often follow rhythmic patterns such as "Don, Dododon, Dododon, Dododon, Dendon (and repeat)." Common chants include: - "Chōsaja" (or "Yoissaja") - "Sōrya, Sōrya" - "Yossai, Yossai" - "Yōsaja, Yoiyoisaja" - "Bērabēra, Berasshossho"
The dragon, symbolizing rain-bringing deities or messengers of the gods, is often featured in taikodai decoration. The fabric straps used to secure the "futon" (mattress-like tiers) atop the float are embroidered with golden dragons, and tassels on the straps symbolize rain. Thus, in regions like [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]] and eastern [[:en:Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]], taikodai have historically been associated more with rainmaking rituals than with mikoshi.
While taikodai do not carry divine spirits like mikoshi, some regions consider the futon or roof portions as sacred vessels where spirits may reside. For this reason, women were traditionally prohibited from touching taikodai in certain areas. Boys who play the drums inside may dress elaborately and avoid touching the ground, much like traditional {{ill|Chigo|ja|稚児|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chigo|qq=}}.
Taikodai are distinct from hikiyama floats in that they are carried rather than pulled. This makes them easier to maneuver in mountainous areas or over uneven terrain. They are often raised high overhead in displays known as "sashiage" (差し上げ) and may also be shaken, rotated, or competed in lifting contests.
== Structure == [[File:Nadanokenkamatsuri-yatai42.jpg|thumb|150px|Interior drum of a taikodai at the {{ill|Nada Fighting Festival|ja|灘のけんか祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nada Fighting Festival|1=Nada no kenka matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566794}}]] [[File:大阪 堺 西湊 ふとん太鼓.jpg|thumb|200px|Futon Taiko from [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]], Osaka Prefecture]]
- The base, called the "taiwa" (台輪), supports the drum, carrying poles, railings, and vertical pillars, with a roof of futon or heavy tiers. - The futon (重) tiers vary from 2 to 8 in number, depending on regional tradition. - Ornamental features such as tassels, decorative straps, and embroidered dragons are common. - Modern taikodai may use lightweight materials for ease of transport.
For further details on specific styles, see: - {{ill|Futon Taiko|ja|ふとん太鼓|1=Futon Daiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futon Taiko|13=User:Immanuelle/Futon Daiko|qq=}} - {{ill|Taikodai|lt=Taikodai|lt=Chōsa|ja|頂載|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōsa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Nada Fighting Festival|ja|灘のけんか祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nada Fighting Festival|1=Nada no kenka matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566794}} * {{ill|Niihama Taiko Festival|ja|新居浜太鼓祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niihama Taiko Festival|1=Niihama Taiko Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501940}} * {{ill|Chōsa.net|ja|ちょうさドットＮＥＴ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōsa.net|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
