== Overview of Taikodai == [[File:甲八幡神社 秋祭り 本宮 甲池に並ぶ屋台.jpg|thumb|View of portable shrine roof floats (taikodai) lined up at {{ill|Kōhachiman Shrine|ja|甲八幡神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōhachiman Shrine|1=Kabuto-hachiman Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q127427418}} in [[:en:Himeji|Himeji]], Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[File:Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine Mikoshi.JPG|thumb|Taikodai ({{ill|portable shrine entry|ja|宮入り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/portable shrine entry|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神幸祭|1=shinkō-sai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123907414}}) at [[:en:Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Hyōgo)|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki)]] in [[:en:Miki|Miki]], Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[File:Niihamataikomatsuri02.jpg|thumb|Taikodai at the {{ill|Niihama Taiko Festival|ja|新居浜太鼓祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niihama Taiko Festival|1=Niihama Taiko Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501940}} (part of the {{ill|Three Great Fighting Festivals|ja|日本三大喧嘩祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Great Fighting Festivals|1=Three Great Fighting Festivals|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17211680}}) in [[:en:Niihama|Niihama]], Ehime Prefecture]] [[File:Nakamozu-cho Futon-Daiko 002.webm|thumb|Taikodai (Futon Daiko) in [[Nakamozu]], [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]], Osaka Prefecture]]
Taikodai are especially common along the [[:en:Seto Inland Sea|Seto Inland Sea]] region and throughout western Japan. While their specific designs vary by region, the general practice involves 2–6 people entering the float to play the drum inside, while tens to over a hundred people carry it, shouting rhythmic chants as they parade. alternate_name include '''Taiko''', '''Futon Taiko''', '''Chōsa''', '''Chōsai''' (頂載), '''Senzairaku''' (千歳楽), '''Yassa''', '''Yotsutaiko''' (四つ太鼓), '''Futon Mikoshi''' (布団御輿), '''Mikoshi Taiko''', '''Futon Dashi''' (布団だんじり), '''Taiko Yama''' (太鼓山), and others. As a collective term, they are also called '''Yatai''' (屋台) or '''Taiko Yatai''' (太鼓屋台).
Unlike [[:en:Mikoshi|mikoshi]], which are owned by the shrine, taikodai are usually provided by the community and serve to escort or lead the mikoshi during a [[:en:Shinko-shiki|Shinkōsai]], as well as to offer performances before the shrine as an act of [[:en:Votive offering|devotion]].
=== Origins and Chants === The name "taikodai" highlights the drum's central role in the procession. The drumming pattern and accompanying chants vary by region but often follow rhythmic patterns such as "Don, Dododon, Dododon, Dododon, Dendon (and repeat)." Common chants include: - "Chōsaja" (or "Yoissaja") - "Sōrya, Sōrya" - "Yossai, Yossai" - "Yōsaja, Yoiyoisaja" - "Bērabēra, Berasshossho"
The dragon, symbolizing rain-bringing deities or messengers of the gods, is often featured in taikodai decoration. The fabric straps used to secure the "futon" (mattress-like tiers) atop the float are embroidered with golden dragons, and tassels on the straps symbolize rain. Thus, in regions like [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]] and eastern [[:en:Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]], taikodai have historically been associated more with rainmaking rituals than with mikoshi.
While taikodai do not carry divine spirits like mikoshi, some regions consider the futon or roof portions as sacred vessels where spirits may reside. For this reason, women were traditionally prohibited from touching taikodai in certain areas. Boys who play the drums inside may dress elaborately and avoid touching the ground, much like traditional {{ill|Chigo|ja|稚児|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chigo|qq=}}.
Taikodai are distinct from hikiyama floats in that they are carried rather than pulled. This makes them easier to maneuver in mountainous areas or over uneven terrain. They are often raised high overhead in displays known as "sashiage" (差し上げ) and may also be shaken, rotated, or competed in lifting contests.
== Structure == [[File:Nadanokenkamatsuri-yatai42.jpg|thumb|150px|Interior drum of a taikodai at the {{ill|Nada Fighting Festival|ja|灘のけんか祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nada Fighting Festival|1=Nada no kenka matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566794}}]] [[File:大阪 堺 西湊 ふとん太鼓.jpg|thumb|200px|Futon Taiko from [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]], Osaka Prefecture]]
- The base, called the "taiwa" (台輪), supports the drum, carrying poles, railings, and vertical pillars, with a roof of futon or heavy tiers. - The futon (重) tiers vary from 2 to 8 in number, depending on regional tradition. - Ornamental features such as tassels, decorative straps, and embroidered dragons are common. - Modern taikodai may use lightweight materials for ease of transport.
For further details on specific styles, see: - {{ill|Futon Taiko|ja|ふとん太鼓|1=Futon Daiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futon Taiko|13=User:Immanuelle/Futon Daiko|qq=}} - {{ill|Taikodai|lt=Taikodai|lt=Chōsa|ja|頂載|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōsa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Nada Fighting Festival|ja|灘のけんか祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nada Fighting Festival|1=Nada no kenka matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566794}} * {{ill|Niihama Taiko Festival|ja|新居浜太鼓祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niihama Taiko Festival|1=Niihama Taiko Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501940}} * {{ill|Chōsa.net|ja|ちょうさドットＮＥＴ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōsa.net|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太鼓台]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太鼓台]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太鼓台]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11444734]] Wikidata name: taikodai [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=March 2019}} '''Taikodai''' (太鼓台) refers to:
1. A platform used to place a [[:en:Drum|taiko]] drum. 2. A type of [[:en:Matsuri float|festival float]] used in [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] festivals. It typically houses a drum inside and is carried or pulled in accordance with the rhythm of the drum. There are two main types: '''Kakiyama''' (舁き山, carried floats) and '''Hikiyama''' (曳き山, pulled floats).

While their specific designs vary by region, the general practice involves 2–6 people entering the float to play the drum inside, while tens to over a hundred people carry it, shouting rhythmic chants as they parade. alternate_name include '''Taiko''', '''Futon Taiko''', '''Chōsa''', '''Chōsai''' (頂載), '''Senzairaku''' (千歳楽), '''Yassa''', '''Yotsutaiko''' (四つ太鼓), '''Futon Mikoshi''' (布団御輿), '''Mikoshi Taiko''', '''Futon Dashi''' (布団だんじり), '''Taiko Yama''' (太鼓山), and others. Unlike {{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|WD=Q979940|lt=mikoshi}}, which are owned by the shrine, taikodai are usually provided by the community and serve to escort or lead the mikoshi during a {{ill|Shinko-shiki|en|Shinko-shiki|WD=Q123907414|lt=Shinkōsai}}, as well as to offer performances before the shrine as an act of {{ill|Votive offering|en|Votive offering|WD=Q2601788|lt=devotion}}.
=== Origins and Chants === The name "taikodai" highlights the drum's central role in the procession. The drumming pattern and accompanying chants vary by region but often follow rhythmic patterns such as "Don, Dododon, Dododon, Dododon, Dendon (and repeat)." Common chants include: - "Chōsaja" (or "Yoissaja") - "Sōrya, Sōrya" - "Yossai, Yossai" - "Yōsaja, Yoiyoisaja" - "Bērabēra, Berasshossho"

The fabric straps used to secure the "futon" (mattress-like tiers) atop the float are embroidered with golden dragons, and tassels on the straps symbolize rain. Thus, in regions like {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|en|Kagawa Prefecture|WD=Q161454|lt=Kagawa Prefecture}} and eastern {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|en|Ehime Prefecture|WD=Q123376|lt=Ehime Prefecture}}, taikodai have historically been associated more with rainmaking rituals than with mikoshi.
While taikodai do not carry divine spirits like mikoshi, some regions consider the futon or roof portions as sacred vessels where spirits may reside. For this reason, women were traditionally prohibited from touching taikodai in certain areas. Boys who play the drums inside may dress elaborately and avoid touching the ground, much like traditional {{ill|Chigo|ja|稚児|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chigo|qq=}}.
Taikodai are distinct from hikiyama floats in that they are carried rather than pulled. This makes them easier to maneuver in mountainous areas or over uneven terrain. They are often raised high overhead in displays known as "sashiage" (差し上げ) and may also be shaken, rotated, or competed in lifting contests.

== Overview of Taikodai == [[File:甲八幡神社 秋祭り 本宮 甲池に並ぶ屋台.jpg|thumb|View of portable shrine roof floats (taikodai) lined up at {{ill|Kōhachiman Shrine|ja|甲八幡神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōhachiman Shrine|1=Kabuto-hachiman Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q127427418}} in {{ill|Himeji|en|Himeji|WD=Q209801|lt=Himeji}}, Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[File:Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine Mikoshi.JPG|thumb|Taikodai ({{ill|portable shrine entry|ja|宮入り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/portable shrine entry|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神幸祭|1=shinkō-sai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123907414}}) at {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Hyōgo)|en|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Hyōgo)|WD=Q3594036|lt=Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki)}} in {{ill|Miki|en|Miki|WD=Q412983|lt=Miki}}, Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[File:Niihamataikomatsuri02.jpg|thumb|Taikodai at the {{ill|Niihama Taiko Festival|ja|新居浜太鼓祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niihama Taiko Festival|1=Niihama Taiko Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501940}} (part of the {{ill|Three Great Fighting Festivals|ja|日本三大喧嘩祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Great Fighting Festivals|1=Three Great Fighting Festivals|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17211680}}) in {{ill|Niihama|en|Niihama|WD=Q386628|lt=Niihama}}, Ehime Prefecture]] [[File:Nakamozu-cho Futon-Daiko 002.webm|thumb|Taikodai (Futon Daiko) in [[Nakamozu]], {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|WD=Q193428|lt=Sakai}}, Osaka Prefecture]]
Taikodai are especially common along the {{ill|Seto Inland Sea|en|Seto Inland Sea|WD=Q231312|lt=Seto Inland Sea}} region and throughout western Japan. While their specific designs vary by region, the general practice involves 2–6 people entering the float to play the drum inside, while tens to over a hundred people carry it, shouting rhythmic chants as they parade. alternate_name include '''Taiko''', '''Futon Taiko''', '''Chōsa''', '''Chōsai''' (頂載), '''Senzairaku''' (千歳楽), '''Yassa''', '''Yotsutaiko''' (四つ太鼓), '''Futon Mikoshi''' (布団御輿), '''Mikoshi Taiko''', '''Futon Dashi''' (布団だんじり), '''Taiko Yama''' (太鼓山), and others. As a collective term, they are also called '''Yatai''' (屋台) or '''Taiko Yatai''' (太鼓屋台).
Unlike {{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|WD=Q979940|lt=mikoshi}}, which are owned by the shrine, taikodai are usually provided by the community and serve to escort or lead the mikoshi during a {{ill|Shinko-shiki|en|Shinko-shiki|WD=Q123907414|lt=Shinkōsai}}, as well as to offer performances before the shrine as an act of {{ill|Votive offering|en|Votive offering|WD=Q2601788|lt=devotion}}.
=== Origins and Chants === The name "taikodai" highlights the drum's central role in the procession. The drumming pattern and accompanying chants vary by region but often follow rhythmic patterns such as "Don, Dododon, Dododon, Dododon, Dendon (and repeat)." Common chants include: - "Chōsaja" (or "Yoissaja") - "Sōrya, Sōrya" - "Yossai, Yossai" - "Yōsaja, Yoiyoisaja" - "Bērabēra, Berasshossho"
The dragon, symbolizing rain-bringing deities or messengers of the gods, is often featured in taikodai decoration. The fabric straps used to secure the "futon" (mattress-like tiers) atop the float are embroidered with golden dragons, and tassels on the straps symbolize rain. Thus, in regions like {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|en|Kagawa Prefecture|WD=Q161454|lt=Kagawa Prefecture}} and eastern {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|en|Ehime Prefecture|WD=Q123376|lt=Ehime Prefecture}}, taikodai have historically been associated more with rainmaking rituals than with mikoshi.
While taikodai do not carry divine spirits like mikoshi, some regions consider the futon or roof portions as sacred vessels where spirits may reside. For this reason, women were traditionally prohibited from touching taikodai in certain areas. Boys who play the drums inside may dress elaborately and avoid touching the ground, much like traditional {{ill|Chigo|ja|稚児|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chigo|qq=}}.
Taikodai are distinct from hikiyama floats in that they are carried rather than pulled. This makes them easier to maneuver in mountainous areas or over uneven terrain. They are often raised high overhead in displays known as "sashiage" (差し上げ) and may also be shaken, rotated, or competed in lifting contests.
== Structure == [[File:Nadanokenkamatsuri-yatai42.jpg|thumb|150px|Interior drum of a taikodai at the {{ill|Nada Fighting Festival|ja|灘のけんか祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nada Fighting Festival|1=Nada no kenka matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566794}}]] [[File:大阪 堺 西湊 ふとん太鼓.jpg|thumb|200px|Futon Taiko from {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|WD=Q193428|lt=Sakai}}, Osaka Prefecture]]
- The base, called the "taiwa" (台輪), supports the drum, carrying poles, railings, and vertical pillars, with a roof of futon or heavy tiers. - The futon (重) tiers vary from 2 to 8 in number, depending on regional tradition. - Ornamental features such as tassels, decorative straps, and embroidered dragons are common. - Modern taikodai may use lightweight materials for ease of transport.
For further details on specific styles, see: - {{ill|Futon Taiko|ja|ふとん太鼓|1=Futon Daiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futon Taiko|13=User:Immanuelle/Futon Daiko|qq=}} - {{ill|Taikodai|lt=Taikodai|lt=Chōsa|ja|頂載|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōsa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Nada Fighting Festival|ja|灘のけんか祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nada Fighting Festival|1=Nada no kenka matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566794}} * {{ill|Niihama Taiko Festival|ja|新居浜太鼓祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niihama Taiko Festival|1=Niihama Taiko Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501940}} * {{ill|Chōsa.net|ja|ちょうさドットＮＥＴ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōsa.net|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

It typically houses a drum inside and is carried or pulled in accordance with the rhythm of the drum. There are two main types: '''Kakiyama''' (舁き山, carried floats) and '''Hikiyama''' (曳き山, pulled floats).
== Overview of Taikodai == [[File:甲八幡神社 秋祭り 本宮 甲池に並ぶ屋台.jpg|thumb|View of portable shrine roof floats (taikodai) lined up at {{ill|Kōhachiman Shrine|ja|甲八幡神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōhachiman Shrine|1=Kabuto-hachiman Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q127427418}} in {{ill|Himeji|en|Himeji|WD=Q209801|lt=Himeji}}, Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[File:Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine Mikoshi.JPG|thumb|Taikodai ({{ill|portable shrine entry|ja|宮入り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/portable shrine entry|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神幸祭|1=shinkō-sai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123907414}}) at {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Hyōgo)|en|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Hyōgo)|WD=Q3594036|lt=Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki)}} in {{ill|Miki|en|Miki|WD=Q412983|lt=Miki}}, Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[File:Niihamataikomatsuri02.jpg|thumb|Taikodai at the {{ill|Niihama Taiko Festival|ja|新居浜太鼓祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niihama Taiko Festival|1=Niihama Taiko Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501940}} (part of the {{ill|Three Great Fighting Festivals|ja|日本三大喧嘩祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Great Fighting Festivals|1=Three Great Fighting Festivals|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17211680}}) in {{ill|Niihama|en|Niihama|WD=Q386628|lt=Niihama}}, Ehime Prefecture]] [[File:Nakamozu-cho Futon-Daiko 002.webm|thumb|Taikodai (Futon Daiko) in [[Nakamozu]], {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|WD=Q193428|lt=Sakai}}, Osaka Prefecture]]
Taikodai are especially common along the {{ill|Seto Inland Sea|en|Seto Inland Sea|WD=Q231312|lt=Seto Inland Sea}} region and throughout western Japan. While their specific designs vary by region, the general practice involves 2–6 people entering the float to play the drum inside, while tens to over a hundred people carry it, shouting rhythmic chants as they parade. alternate_name include '''Taiko''', '''Futon Taiko''', '''Chōsa''', '''Chōsai''' (頂載), '''Senzairaku''' (千歳楽), '''Yassa''', '''Yotsutaiko''' (四つ太鼓), '''Futon Mikoshi''' (布団御輿), '''Mikoshi Taiko''', '''Futon Dashi''' (布団だんじり), '''Taiko Yama''' (太鼓山), and others. As a collective term, they are also called '''Yatai''' (屋台) or '''Taiko Yatai''' (太鼓屋台).
Unlike {{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|WD=Q979940|lt=mikoshi}}, which are owned by the shrine, taikodai are usually provided by the community and serve to escort or lead the mikoshi during a {{ill|Shinko-shiki|en|Shinko-shiki|WD=Q123907414|lt=Shinkōsai}}, as well as to offer performances before the shrine as an act of {{ill|Votive offering|en|Votive offering|WD=Q2601788|lt=devotion}}.
=== Origins and Chants === The name "taikodai" highlights the drum's central role in the procession. The drumming pattern and accompanying chants vary by region but often follow rhythmic patterns such as "Don, Dododon, Dododon, Dododon, Dendon (and repeat)." Common chants include: - "Chōsaja" (or "Yoissaja") - "Sōrya, Sōrya" - "Yossai, Yossai" - "Yōsaja, Yoiyoisaja" - "Bērabēra, Berasshossho"
The dragon, symbolizing rain-bringing deities or messengers of the gods, is often featured in taikodai decoration. The fabric straps used to secure the "futon" (mattress-like tiers) atop the float are embroidered with golden dragons, and tassels on the straps symbolize rain. Thus, in regions like {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|en|Kagawa Prefecture|WD=Q161454|lt=Kagawa Prefecture}} and eastern {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|en|Ehime Prefecture|WD=Q123376|lt=Ehime Prefecture}}, taikodai have historically been associated more with rainmaking rituals than with mikoshi.
While taikodai do not carry divine spirits like mikoshi, some regions consider the futon or roof portions as sacred vessels where spirits may reside. For this reason, women were traditionally prohibited from touching taikodai in certain areas. Boys who play the drums inside may dress elaborately and avoid touching the ground, much like traditional {{ill|Chigo|ja|稚児|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chigo|qq=}}.
Taikodai are distinct from hikiyama floats in that they are carried rather than pulled. This makes them easier to maneuver in mountainous areas or over uneven terrain. They are often raised high overhead in displays known as "sashiage" (差し上げ) and may also be shaken, rotated, or competed in lifting contests.
== Structure == [[File:Nadanokenkamatsuri-yatai42.jpg|thumb|150px|Interior drum of a taikodai at the {{ill|Nada Fighting Festival|ja|灘のけんか祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nada Fighting Festival|1=Nada no kenka matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566794}}]] [[File:大阪 堺 西湊 ふとん太鼓.jpg|thumb|200px|Futon Taiko from {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|WD=Q193428|lt=Sakai}}, Osaka Prefecture]]
- The base, called the "taiwa" (台輪), supports the drum, carrying poles, railings, and vertical pillars, with a roof of futon or heavy tiers. - The futon (重) tiers vary from 2 to 8 in number, depending on regional tradition. - Ornamental features such as tassels, decorative straps, and embroidered dragons are common. - Modern taikodai may use lightweight materials for ease of transport.
For further details on specific styles, see: - {{ill|Futon Taiko|ja|ふとん太鼓|1=Futon Daiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futon Taiko|13=User:Immanuelle/Futon Daiko|qq=}} - {{ill|Taikodai|lt=Taikodai|lt=Chōsa|ja|頂載|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōsa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Nada Fighting Festival|ja|灘のけんか祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nada Fighting Festival|1=Nada no kenka matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566794}} * {{ill|Niihama Taiko Festival|ja|新居浜太鼓祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niihama Taiko Festival|1=Niihama Taiko Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501940}} * {{ill|Chōsa.net|ja|ちょうさドットＮＥＴ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōsa.net|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
{{Japanese festivals}}
{{translated page|ja|太鼓台|version=103633101|comment=Imported full ja history}}

It typically houses a drum inside and is carried or pulled in accordance with the rhythm of the drum. There are two main types: '''Kakiyama''' (舁き山, carried floats) and '''Hikiyama''' (曳き山, pulled floats).
== Overview of Taikodai == [[File:甲八幡神社 秋祭り 本宮 甲池に並ぶ屋台.jpg|thumb|View of portable shrine roof floats (taikodai) lined up at {{ill|Kōhachiman Shrine|ja|甲八幡神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōhachiman Shrine|1=Kabuto-hachiman Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q127427418}} in {{ill|Himeji|en|Himeji|WD=Q209801|lt=Himeji|1=Himeji}}, Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[File:Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine Mikoshi.JPG|thumb|Taikodai ({{ill|portable shrine entry|ja|宮入り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/portable shrine entry|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神幸祭|1=Shinko-shiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123907414}}) at {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Hyōgo)|en|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Hyōgo)|WD=Q3594036|lt=Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki)|1=Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Hyōgo)}} in {{ill|Miki|en|Miki|WD=Q412983|lt=Miki|1=Miki}}, Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[File:Niihamataikomatsuri02.jpg|thumb|Taikodai at the {{ill|Niihama Taiko Festival|ja|新居浜太鼓祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niihama Taiko Festival|1=Niihama Taiko Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11501940}} (part of the {{ill|Three Great Fighting Festivals|ja|日本三大喧嘩祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Great Fighting Festivals|1=Q17211680|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17211680}}) in {{ill|Niihama|en|Niihama|WD=Q386628|lt=Niihama|1=Niihama}}, Ehime Prefecture]] [[File:Nakamozu-cho Futon-Daiko 002.webm|thumb|Taikodai (Futon Daiko) in [[Nakamozu]], {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|WD=Q193428|lt=Sakai|1=Sakai}}, Osaka Prefecture]]
Taikodai are especially common along the {{ill|Seto Inland Sea|en|Seto Inland Sea|WD=Q231312|lt=Seto Inland Sea|1=Seto Inland Sea}} region and throughout western Japan. While their specific designs vary by region, the general practice involves 2–6 people entering the float to play the drum inside, while tens to over a hundred people carry it, shouting rhythmic chants as they parade. alternate_name include '''Taiko''', '''Futon Taiko''', '''Chōsa''', '''Chōsai''' (頂載), '''Senzairaku''' (千歳楽), '''Yassa''', '''Yotsutaiko''' (四つ太鼓), '''Futon Mikoshi''' (布団御輿), '''Mikoshi Taiko''', '''Futon Dashi''' (布団だんじり), '''Taiko Yama''' (太鼓山), and others. As a collective term, they are also called '''Yatai''' (屋台) or '''Taiko Yatai''' (太鼓屋台).
Unlike {{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|WD=Q979940|lt=mikoshi|1=Mikoshi}}, which are owned by the shrine, taikodai are usually provided by the community and serve to escort or lead the mikoshi during a {{ill|Shinko-shiki|en|Shinko-shiki|WD=Q123907414|lt=Shinkōsai|1=Shinko-shiki}}, as well as to offer performances before the shrine as an act of {{ill|Votive offering|en|Votive offering|WD=Q2601788|lt=devotion|1=Votive offering}}.
=== Origins and Chants === The name "taikodai" highlights the drum's central role in the procession. The drumming pattern and accompanying chants vary by region but often follow rhythmic patterns such as "Don, Dododon, Dododon, Dododon, Dendon (and repeat)." Common chants include: - "Chōsaja" (or "Yoissaja") - "Sōrya, Sōrya" - "Yossai, Yossai" - "Yōsaja, Yoiyoisaja" - "Bērabēra, Berasshossho"
The dragon, symbolizing rain-bringing deities or messengers of the gods, is often featured in taikodai decoration. The fabric straps used to secure the "futon" (mattress-like tiers) atop the float are embroidered with golden dragons, and tassels on the straps symbolize rain. Thus, in regions like {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|en|Kagawa Prefecture|WD=Q161454|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|1=Kagawa Prefecture}} and eastern {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|en|Ehime Prefecture|WD=Q123376|lt=Ehime Prefecture|1=Ehime Prefecture}}, taikodai have historically been associated more with rainmaking rituals than with mikoshi.
While taikodai do not carry divine spirits like mikoshi, some regions consider the futon or roof portions as sacred vessels where spirits may reside. For this reason, women were traditionally prohibited from touching taikodai in certain areas. Boys who play the drums inside may dress elaborately and avoid touching the ground, much like traditional {{ill|Chigo|ja|稚児|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chigo|qq=}}.
Taikodai are distinct from hikiyama floats in that they are carried rather than pulled. This makes them easier to maneuver in mountainous areas or over uneven terrain. They are often raised high overhead in displays known as "sashiage" (差し上げ) and may also be shaken, rotated, or competed in lifting contests.
== Structure == [[File:Nadanokenkamatsuri-yatai42.jpg|thumb|150px|Interior drum of a taikodai at the {{ill|Nada Fighting Festival|ja|灘のけんか祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nada Fighting Festival|1=Nada no kenka matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566794}}]] [[File:大阪 堺 西湊 ふとん太鼓.jpg|thumb|200px|Futon Taiko from {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|WD=Q193428|lt=Sakai|1=Sakai}}, Osaka Prefecture]]
- The base, called the "taiwa" (台輪), supports the drum, carrying poles, railings, and vertical pillars, with a roof of futon or heavy tiers. - The futon (重) tiers vary from 2 to 8 in number, depending on regional tradition. - Ornamental features such as tassels, decorative straps, and embroidered dragons are common. - Modern taikodai may use lightweight materials for ease of transport.
For further details on specific styles, see: - {{ill|Futon Taiko|ja|ふとん太鼓|1=Futon Daiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futon Taiko|13=User:Immanuelle/Futon Daiko|qq=}} - {{ill|Taikodai|lt=Taikodai|lt=Chōsa|ja|頂載|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōsa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
